Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: Acceptance Criteria are clear statements that explain what a feature or requirement must do so everyone understands when the work is finished and correct, and they are created by gathering information, writing details, and making sure all needs are included and easy to check.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian: "How to write acceptance criteria"  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/user-stories/acceptance-criteria

2. Scrum.org: "Acceptance Criteria: Definition, Examples, and Best Practices"  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/acceptance-criteria-definition-examples-and-best-practices

3. Mountain Goat Software: "Acceptance Criteria and User Stories"  
https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/acceptance-criteria-and-user-stories
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Define what the feature should do by talking with everyone who knows about the product and writing down the main idea.
2. Gather and improve the details about what is needed for the feature by asking questions, listening, and making sure nothing is missed.
3. Write clear Acceptance Criteria that explain exactly what the feature must do so everyone understands, using good communication, problem-solving, and careful checking, while making sure all duties are informed and can give advice.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Acceptance Criteria are valuable because they clearly explain what needs to be done and how everyone will know when the work is finished, helping people with different duties understand what is expected so they can work together and reach the milestone successfully.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete the Acceptance Criteria, the Product Ownership and Business Analysis duties are accountable for making sure everything is correct and finished, while Product Ownership and Business Analysis are also responsible for doing the work like defining the feature vision, gathering and refining requirements, and writing the Acceptance Criteria. Product Management, Business Analysis, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Scrum Master, Project Management, Solutions Architecture, Development, QA Analysis, and Information Security Analysis are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge during the process. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed so everyone knows what is happening. The duties use skills like problem-solving, teamwork, attention to detail, and communication, and follow practices such as decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the Requirements milestone together.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: An Accessibility Analysis is a report that checks how easy it is for everyone, including people with disabilities, to use a website or app by measuring things like how clear the words are, how easy it is to move around, and if special tools work with it, using careful thinking, good communication, and working with others to make sure everyone can use the product.
Online Resources:
1. WebAIM: Accessibility Evaluation Tutorials  
https://webaim.org/articles/evaluating/

2. W3C Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI): Evaluation and Testing  
https://www.w3.org/WAI/test-evaluate/

3. Deque University: Accessibility Testing Tutorials  
https://dequeuniversity.com/tutorials/testing
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The UX Design duty works with other duties to gather everyone’s ideas and make sure all the work fits together, while Business Analysis checks that the Accessibility Analysis is done correctly and meets the goals. 
2. The UX Strategy duty measures how easy things are to use and plans ways to make them better for everyone, getting advice from duties like UX Research, UX Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis. 
3. All duties are kept informed about what is happening, and everyone uses skills like paying attention to details, solving problems, and working together, while following good practices like making decisions, taking responsibility, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so the Accessibility Analysis is done well at each important step, such as Experience Design, Acceptance Testing, Quality Assurance, and Launch.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Accessibility Analysis is valuable because it helps everyone understand if a product can be used by people with different abilities, making sure no one is left out. By measuring accessibility, people doing duties like business analysis, UX research, and UX writing can find and fix problems early, which helps reach important steps like experience design, acceptance testing, quality assurance, and launch. This process uses skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, and teamwork, and supports good practices like decision-making, ownership, and making sure everyone feels safe and included.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, UX (User Experience) Design, works with other duties to complete an Accessibility Analysis by making sure everyone’s ideas and work fit together. Business Analysis is accountable, making sure the Accessibility Analysis is done right and meets the goals. UX (User Experience) Strategy is responsible for doing the main work, like measuring accessibility and planning how to make things easier for everyone to use. UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted, which means they give advice and share their knowledge to help make the Accessibility Analysis better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed, so they know what is happening and can stay updated. The work uses skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, and teamwork, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to make sure the Accessibility Analyses are done well at each milestone, such as Experience Design, Acceptance Testing, Quality Assurance, and Launch.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: An Affinity Map is a way to organize ideas and information from research by grouping similar thoughts together, which helps people see patterns and make better decisions about what users need and how to improve products.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Affinity Diagramming: How to Cluster Your Ideas and Reveal Insights"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/affinity-diagram/

2. Miro Blog – "How to Create an Affinity Diagram: Step-by-Step Guide"  
https://miro.com/guides/affinity-diagram/

3. Interaction Design Foundation – "Affinity Diagrams: How to Cluster and Analyze UX Research Data"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/affinity-diagrams-how-to-cluster-and-analyze-ux-research-data
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information by doing primary and secondary research and making a research plan with the help of duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy. 
2. Sort and group the research findings into categories by working together, using attention to detail, problem-solving, and good decision-making, while the UX Research duty leads the process and asks for advice and feedback from other duties. 
3. Share the finished Affinity Map with everyone involved, keep them updated on progress, and use the map to help with continuous discovery and making better choices for the product.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Affinity Map helps people doing different duties organize lots of ideas and research findings into clear groups, so everyone can see patterns and understand what matters most, which makes it easier to make good decisions and keep learning new things during Continuous Discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: An Affinity Map is made when the duty owner, UX (User Experience) Research, works with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, and UX (User Experience) Strategy by doing activities such as primary and secondary research and creating research plans, using skills like attention to detail, teamwork, and problem-solving, and following practices like decision-making and ownership; UX (User Experience) Research is accountable and responsible for making the Affinity Map, while Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing are consulted to give advice and feedback, and all other duties are kept informed about the progress and results as the milestone of continuous discovery is reached.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: An Agile Maturity Measurements is a way to check how well a group is using agile methods by looking at how they work together, make decisions, solve problems, and keep growing, and it is done by leading meetings, helping people talk and listen, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, and making sure everyone is working toward the same goals.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org – "How to Measure Agile Maturity"  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/how-measure-agile-maturity

2. Atlassian Agile Coach – "Agile Maturity Model"  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/agile-at-scale/agile-maturity-model

3. Mountain Goat Software – "Agile Maturity Assessment: How to Measure and Improve Your Agile Process"  
https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/agile-maturity-assessment-how-to-measure-and-improve-your-agile-process
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. The Scrum Master leads the group by planning and running Sprint Retrospectives, using skills like speaking clearly, helping others talk, and solving problems together.  
2. The Scrum Master and Agile Coaching make sure Agile Maturity Measurements are finished, while the Scrum Master does the main work, asking for advice from Product Management, UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development duties.  
3. Everyone uses good habits like making choices together, taking responsibility, helping each other, making everyone feel safe to share, and giving people the power to help, so all product milestones are reached and Agile Maturity Measurements are done well.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Agile Maturity Measurements are valuable because they help everyone see how well the group is working together, spot what is going well, and find areas to improve, so the group can reach each product milestone faster and with better results.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The Scrum Master is the duty owner for Agile Maturity Measurements and works with all other duties by leading activities like Sprint Retrospective Facilitation, using skills such as meeting facilitation, public speaking, and problem-solving. The Scrum Master and Agile Coaching are accountable for making sure Agile Maturity Measurements are completed, while the Scrum Master is responsible for doing the work. Many duties, including Product Management, UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, and all duties are kept informed about progress and results. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to help reach all product milestones and make sure Agile Maturity Measurements are done well.

Deliverable Description: An Agile Method is a way to organize and finish work by breaking big tasks into smaller steps, making decisions together, and always looking for ways to improve, while using skills like problem-solving, communication, and time management to help everyone feel safe, included, and responsible for their part.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org - https://www.scrum.org/resources
2. Agile Alliance - https://www.agilealliance.org/agile101/
3. Atlassian Agile Coach - https://www.atlassian.com/agile
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by leading everyone in choosing which Agile Method to use, making sure all duties share their ideas and advice.  
2. Guide the group through important steps, like the Intake milestone, by planning what needs to be done and making sure everyone knows their part.  
3. Use skills like problem-solving, working together, and clear communication, and follow good practices like making decisions, taking responsibility, and making sure everyone feels safe to share, so all duties can help finish the Agile Method the right way.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Agile Method is valuable because it helps everyone work together better by making sure people share ideas, solve problems quickly, and make good decisions so the group can start projects the right way and reach the Intake milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete an Agile Method, the Product Operations duty owner works with all other duties by leading activities like choosing an Agile Method and guiding the group through important steps called milestones, such as Intake. Product Operations, Agile Coaching, and Scrum Master are accountable, making sure everything is done right, while Product Operations is responsible for doing the work. Many other duties, like UX Design, Development, and Business Analysis, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, and everyone is kept informed about what is happening. All duties use skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follow practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to help everyone work well together and reach the goal.

Deliverable Description: An AI Prompt is a set of clear instructions or questions you write to help a computer program understand what you want it to do, and creating AI Prompts means you need to think carefully, organize your ideas, work well with others, and use good communication so the computer gives you the best answers.
Online Resources:
1. OpenAI Cookbook – https://github.com/openai/openai-cookbook  
2. DeepLearning.AI Prompt Engineering Course – https://www.deeplearning.ai/short-courses/chatgpt-prompt-engineering-for-developers/  
3. Learn Prompting – https://learnprompting.org/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Each duty shares ideas and talks about what the AI Prompt should do and how it should work.  
2. Duties work together to write, organize, and check the AI Prompt, making sure it is clear, correct, and helpful.  
3. Duties help each other solve problems, keep everyone updated about changes, and make sure the AI Prompt is finished well and on time by using good communication, paying attention to details, and following practices like being responsible, helping others, and making everyone feel safe to share ideas.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An AI Prompt is valuable because it helps everyone understand exactly what needs to be done, making it easier for people with different duties to work together and reach the goal faster. By creating clear AI Prompts, people can share ideas, solve problems, and make good decisions, which helps everyone stay organized and finish each product milestone successfully.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: All duties work together cross-functionally to create AI Prompts by sharing ideas, making decisions, and helping each other with activities like writing, organizing, and solving problems. Each duty is accountable and responsible for making sure the AI Prompts are done well and on time, while also being consulted and informed about progress and changes. Duties use skills like attention to detail, communication, and teamwork, and follow practices such as ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach all product milestones.

Deliverable Description: An Analytics Audit is when you carefully collect and study data to find out how well something is working, using math and statistics to spot patterns and make smart choices, while being organized, working with others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas.
Online Resources:
1. Google Analytics Help Center – Analytics Audit Guide  
https://support.google.com/analytics/answer/1009694

2. MeasureSchool – How to Perform a Google Analytics Audit (YouTube Tutorial)  
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6QwQvQnQn1A

https://www.lovesdata.com/blog/google-analytics-audit
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. First, the UX Research duty collects all the analytics data needed and checks that the information is correct and complete. 
2. Next, the UX Research duty calculates statistics from the data, using careful attention and problem-solving skills, while asking for advice from Product Ownership, Product Management, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing. 
3. Finally, everyone involved is kept updated about the progress, and the work is checked through Acceptance Testing and Continuous Discovery, using good practices like making smart decisions, taking responsibility, helping others, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, and letting people do their best work.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Analytics Audit is valuable because it helps people doing different duties understand how well things are working by collecting and checking important numbers and facts. This helps everyone make better choices, find problems early, and make sure the product is easy to use and meets everyone’s needs, which is important for reaching goals like Acceptance Testing and Continuous Discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: An Analytics Audit happens when the UX (User Experience) Research duty leads the work and makes sure everything is done right, while Product Ownership also checks that the work meets the goals. UX (User Experience) Research is responsible for collecting analytics and calculating statistics, using skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, and teamwork. Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing are consulted to give advice and share ideas. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress. The main activities are collecting analytics and calculating statistics, and the important milestones are Acceptance Testing and Continuous Discovery. Everyone uses good practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to make sure the Analytics Audits are done well.

Deliverable Description: An API Library is a set of instructions and tools that help people connect different computer programs, and to make one you need to write code for both the website and the server, pay close attention to details, solve problems, use logic, and work well with others while making good decisions and staying organized.

Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Plan what the API Library should do by talking with duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and UX Design to understand the needs and goals.  
2. Build the API Library by writing front-end and server-side code, using problem-solving, attention to detail, and good decision-making, while making sure to follow safe and smart coding practices.  
3. Test the API Library with help from QA Analysis and Information Security Analysis, get advice from Solutions Architecture, and keep all duties updated about progress, especially when big steps like Architecture and Development are finished.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An API Library is valuable because it lets people doing different duties, like coding the front and back of a website, work together easily by sharing clear instructions for how programs talk to each other, which helps everyone build and test things faster and reach the goals for making and planning the product.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To make an API Library, the Development duty is the owner and is both accountable and responsible for building and coding it, using skills like problem-solving, attention to detail, and teamwork, while following good practices like decision-making and ownership. Product Ownership and Development are accountable for making sure the API Library meets the goals, and Development is responsible for doing the actual coding and building. Other duties, like Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, Solutions Architecture, Information Security Analysis, QA Analysis, and Project Management, are consulted to give advice and help with planning, design, security, and testing. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress and important updates, especially at big milestones like Architecture and Development.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: Architecture Diagrams are drawings that show how different parts of a system work together, helping people understand how to build, organize, and connect everything needed for a project.
Online Resources:
1. Microsoft Learn – Azure Architecture Center  
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/guide/architecture-diagrams/

2. AWS Architecture Center  
https://aws.amazon.com/architecture/

3. Lucidchart Blog – How to Make an Architecture Diagram  
https://www.lucidchart.com/blog/how-to-make-an-architecture-diagram
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The Solutions Architecture duty starts by talking with Business Analysis, Development, and Product Ownership duties to understand what the project needs and gathers ideas and feedback from them. 
2. Next, Solutions Architecture uses problem-solving, communication, and decision-making skills to draw the Architecture Diagrams, making sure to include all the important technical details and plans. 
3. Finally, Solutions Architecture checks the diagrams with Product Ownership to make sure they are correct and finished on time, keeps everyone else like QA Analysis, Graphic Design, and Information Security Analysis updated, and makes sure the diagrams meet what everyone needs.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Architecture Diagram is valuable because it shows how all the parts of a project fit together, making it easier for everyone with different duties like business analysis, development, and product ownership to understand what needs to be built and how to make good decisions, solve problems, and stay organized so the project can reach the Architecture milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the Architecture Diagrams, the Solutions Architecture duty is responsible for making the diagrams and is also one of the duties accountable, along with Product Ownership, for making sure they are correct and finished on time. Solutions Architecture works with Business Analysis, Development, and Product Ownership, who are all consulted to give ideas and feedback, while everyone else, like QA Analysis, Graphic Design, and Information Security Analysis, is kept informed about the progress. The main activities are making the technical plans, using skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and following practices like decision-making, ownership, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. The big milestone is when the Architecture Diagrams are ready and meet the needs of everyone involved.

Deliverable Description: Behavior-Driven Development Scenarios are clear stories that explain how a product should work by showing what happens when someone uses it, helping everyone understand what is needed, checking if it works right, and making sure everyone agrees on what to build.
Online Resources:
1. Cucumber Documentation – https://cucumber.io/docs/bdd/
2. ThoughtWorks Insights: BDD – https://www.thoughtworks.com/insights/blog/introduction-behavior-driven-development
3. Ministry of Testing: BDD Resources – https://www.ministryoftesting.com/dojo/series/bdd-resources
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Write clear and simple Behavior-Driven Development Scenarios by using good problem-solving, communication, and teamwork skills, making sure the requirements are easy to understand and meet what everyone needs.
2. Ask for advice and knowledge from other duties like UX Design, Product Management, and Development when writing requirements and setting acceptance criteria, so the scenarios are correct and helpful.
3. Keep all duties informed about progress and important decisions as you move through requirements, quality assurance, and launch milestones, using practices like ownership, decision-making, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Behavior-Driven Development Scenarios are valuable because they help everyone understand exactly what needs to happen for a project to work, making it easier to spot problems early, make good decisions, and make sure the final product meets what people want, which helps reach important goals like having clear requirements, checking for quality, and getting ready to launch.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: Business Analysis is accountable and responsible for making sure the Behavior-Driven Development Scenarios are written clearly and meet the needs of everyone, while other duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge during activities like writing requirements and setting acceptance criteria; all duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, and Account Management, are kept informed about progress and important decisions, so everyone knows what is happening as the requirements, quality assurance, and launch milestones are reached, using skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and following practices like ownership, decision-making, and psychological safety.

Deliverable Description: A Brand Archetypes project is when you use creativity, problem-solving, and good communication to help form a brand’s identity by choosing the right personality and style for it, making sure every detail fits together, and working carefully through each step like research, design, and writing to make the brand clear and memorable.
Online Resources:
1. HubSpot – "The 12 Brand Archetypes & How to Use Them"  
https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/brand-archetypes

2. Iconic Fox – "Brand Archetypes: The Complete Guide"  
https://iconicfox.com.au/brand-archetypes/

3. Branding Strategy Insider – "Brand Archetypes And Their Use In Branding"  
https://www.brandingstrategyinsider.com/brand-archetypes-and-their-use-in-branding/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information about the brand’s goals, values, and audience by talking with different duties like Business Analysis and Product Management. 
2. Use creativity and attention to detail to write and shape the Brand Archetypes, making sure they fit the brand’s identity and what the brand wants to say, while getting advice from duties like UX Research and UX Design. 
3. Share the work with all duties, listen to feedback, and make changes as needed, using good decision-making and ownership, so the Brand Archetypes are clear and ready for the next steps in Experience Design.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Brand Archetypes are valuable because they help everyone doing their duty understand what the brand stands for and how it should feel to people, making it easier to make good choices and create a better experience for users. This helps reach the milestone by making sure all the work matches the brand’s identity and goals, so everything feels connected and clear.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, UX (User Experience) Writing, works with other duties to create the Brand Archetypes by using skills like creativity, attention to detail, and teamwork. Product Management and UX (User Experience) Writing are accountable for making sure the Brand Archetypes are done right, while UX (User Experience) Writing is responsible for doing the main work. Duties like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and Product Management are consulted to give advice and share ideas. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others, are kept informed about the progress. Everyone uses good practices like decision-making, ownership, and service leadership to reach important milestones such as Requirements and Experience Design, making sure the Brand Archetypes match the brand’s identity and goals.

Deliverable Description: A Burn Down Chart shows how much work is left to do in a project by tracking progress every day, helping everyone see if things are on schedule, and making it easier to spot problems early so they can be fixed quickly.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian Agile Coach – Burndown Charts  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/burndown-charts

2. Scrum.org – How to Create and Use a Burndown Chart  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/how-create-and-use-burndown-chart

3. Mountain Goat Software – Burndown Charts Explained  
https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/agile/burndown-charts
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. The Scrum Master gathers information from everyone about how much work is left in the sprint and updates the Burn Down Chart every day.  
2. The Scrum Master checks the chart to see if the work is on track and asks for help from Agile Coaching, Business Analysis, and Project Management if there are any problems or questions.  
3. All duties pay attention to the chart, use good communication, and follow helpful practices like being organized, solving problems, and supporting each other so everyone knows how close they are to finishing all the product milestones.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Burn Down Chart is valuable because it shows how much work is left in a project and helps everyone see if the group is on track to finish on time, so people can make smart choices, stay organized, and help each other reach all product milestones.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: A Scrum Master is the duty owner for a Burn Down Chart and works with all other duties to finish the Burn Down Chart by tracking sprint progress and making sure everyone knows how much work is left. The Scrum Master is responsible for updating the chart and making decisions, while Agile Coaching is also accountable for making sure the chart is done right. Business Analysis and Project Management are consulted to give advice and help solve problems. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the Burn Down Chart so everyone knows the progress. To do this, everyone uses skills like attention to detail, organization, problem-solving, and communication, and follows practices like ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, empowerment, agility, and continuous growth to reach all product milestones.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Business Plan is a detailed guide that explains how a business will work, including research about the market, testing prices, and making smart strategies, while using skills like problem-solving, communication, and organization to make good decisions and keep improving.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – How to Write a Great Business Plan  
https://hbr.org/1997/07/how-to-write-a-great-business-plan

2. Bplans – Business Plan Presentation Guide  
https://www.bplans.com/business-presentation/

3. SCORE – How to Present Your Business Plan  
https://www.score.org/resource/blog-post/how-present-your-business-plan
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by doing Market Research, where the Product Management duty leads and asks for help from Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and UX duties to find out what people want and what other businesses are doing. 
2. Next, do Price Testing and Strategy Definition, where Product Management works with others to test different prices and make smart choices about how to sell the product, using advice from all the other duties. 
3. Keep everyone updated, use good communication, solve problems together, and always look for ways to make the Business Plan better by learning new things and sharing ideas, so the plan keeps improving all the time.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Business Plan is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand the goals, the market, and the steps needed to succeed, making it easier to make smart choices, solve problems, and keep improving together so the group can reach the milestone of Continuous Discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Business Plan, the Product Management duty is in charge and makes sure everything gets done right, while also doing most of the work. Other duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, and UX Writing help by giving advice and sharing their knowledge, especially during activities like Market Research, Price Testing, and Strategy Definition. All duties, including ones like Development, Graphic Design, and QA Analysis, are kept updated about what’s happening. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follows good practices like making decisions together, taking responsibility, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. The main milestone is Continuous Discovery, which means everyone keeps learning and improving the Business Plans as they go.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: Buyer Personas are detailed descriptions of different types of customers, created by using customer segmentation and research to understand their needs, behaviors, and preferences, so that people doing product management and user experience research can make better decisions and create products that truly help and serve these customers.
Online Resources:
1. HubSpot: "How to Create Detailed Buyer Personas for Your Business"  
https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/buyer-persona-research

2. Hootsuite: "How to Create Detailed Buyer Personas (with Free Templates)"  
https://blog.hootsuite.com/buyer-persona/

3. Semrush: "How to Create a Buyer Persona (Examples + Free Template)"  
https://www.semrush.com/blog/buyer-persona/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Gather information about different types of customers by talking to them, looking at data, and asking questions, with Product Management making sure everything is done well and on time.  
2. Use problem-solving, communication, and research skills to sort customers into groups based on what they need and like, with help from Product Ownership and UX Research duties who share their knowledge about customers.  
3. Share ideas and updates with all duties, like Business Analysis, Development, and Graphic Design, so everyone knows what is happening and can give advice, making sure the Buyer Personas are clear, accurate, and useful during the Continuous Discovery milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Buyer Personas helps with the duty of Product Ownership and UX Research because it shows who the customers are, what they like, and what they need, so everyone working on the product can make better choices and keep learning about what customers want, which helps reach the Continuous Discovery milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create Buyer Personas, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible for making sure everything gets done right, using skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and good communication. Product Ownership and UX (User Experience) Research duties are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge about what customers need, while other duties like UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and Development are also consulted for their expertise. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and more, are kept informed about progress and important decisions. Everyone works together by sharing ideas, making decisions, and staying organized, especially during the Continuous Discovery milestone, so the Buyer Personas are accurate and helpful.

Deliverable Description: Code is a set of written instructions that tells computers what to do, and creating Code means using special languages to build things like websites, apps, or programs by writing, testing, and fixing these instructions, making sure they work well, are easy to use, and are clearly explained for others to understand.
Online Resources:

Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Plan what the code needs to do by talking with everyone and getting advice from duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and UX Design.  
2. Write the code for the front-end, server-side, and blockchain, create REST APIs, and make sure to document everything clearly, using problem-solving and paying close attention to details.  
3. Deploy the code, test it with help from QA Analysis and Solutions Architecture duties, fix any problems, and keep all duties updated about progress and important changes, making sure everyone can share ideas and help each other.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Code is valuable to cross-functional groups because it brings together different duties like business analysis, product ownership, QA analysis, solutions architecture, and user experience design, helping everyone build, test, and improve the product so the group can reach the development milestone faster and with better quality.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete Code, the Development duty is the owner and is responsible for activities like blockchain coding, creating REST APIs, deploying, documenting, and coding both front-end and server-side parts, using skills such as problem-solving, teamwork, and attention to detail. Product Ownership and Development duties are accountable for making sure Code meets the goals and milestones, while duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, Solutions Architecture, Information Security Analysis, QA Analysis, and Project Management are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress and important updates. Everyone works together by making decisions, showing ownership, helping each other, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Competitive Analysis is when you carefully look at other companies that do something similar to what you want to do, so you can learn what they do well and what you could do better, using skills like paying attention to details, thinking carefully, and working with others to make smart choices and help your product fit what people need.
Online Resources:
1. HubSpot Blog – Competitive Analysis: What It Is and How to Do It  
https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/competitive-analysis-kit

2. SEMrush Academy – Competitive Analysis Course  
https://www.semrush.com/academy/courses/competitive-analysis

3. Harvard Business Review – A Refresher on Competitive Analysis  
https://hbr.org/2015/12/a-refresher-on-competitive-analysis
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Product Management makes sure everyone knows what to do and checks that the Competitive Analysis is done well.  
2. UX Research, Product Ownership, and Product Management look closely at other companies to see what they do, using skills like thinking carefully, looking at data, and working together.  
3. Business Analysis, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, and UX Strategy give helpful advice and ideas to make the Competitive Analysis stronger, while everyone shares updates, makes smart choices, helps each other, and feels safe to share thoughts, all working toward always learning new things.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Competitive Analysis is valuable because it helps people doing different duties understand what others in the market are doing, spot new ideas, and avoid mistakes, so everyone can make better choices and keep improving together to reach the Continuous Discovery milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For a Competitive Analysis, the Product Management duty is in charge and makes sure everything gets done right, while UX Research, Product Ownership, and Product Management do the main work by investigating competitors and using skills like critical thinking, data analysis, and teamwork. Business Analysis, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, and UX Strategy are asked for advice and ideas to make the Competitive Analysis better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept updated about what’s happening. Everyone uses good practices like making smart decisions, taking responsibility, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, all while working toward the milestone of Continuous Discovery.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Component Library is a collection of ready-made design pieces, like buttons and menus, that you create and organize so others can use them to build websites or apps more easily; making one means you need to pay close attention to details, think carefully, work well with others, and use good design rules to make sure everything looks and works great for everyone.
Online Resources:
1. Storybook Documentation – https://storybook.js.org/docs/react/get-started/introduction  
2. Material-UI Component Library Guide – https://mui.com/material-ui/getting-started/overview/  
3. Smashing Magazine: How To Build a Component Library – https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2020/01/design-system-component-library-react/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The UX Design duty works with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, UX Research, UX Strategy, and UX Writing to share ideas and decide what parts to include in the Component Library, using skills like problem-solving, paying close attention to details, and clear communication. 
2. The UX Strategy duty does the main work by designing and creating the components and the UI Kit, while Business Analysis checks that everything is done correctly and matches what is needed. 
3. Other duties, such as Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis, give advice and feedback, and everyone is kept updated about progress, using good practices like making decisions together, taking responsibility, and working safely and respectfully to finish the Experience Design milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Component Library helps people doing different duties work together by giving everyone the same set of building blocks for making designs, so things look and work the same way, which makes it easier and faster to reach the Experience Design milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, UX (User Experience) Design, works with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, and UX (User Experience) Writing to create the Component Library by sharing ideas, making decisions, and using skills like problem-solving, attention to detail, and good communication. Business Analysis is accountable for making sure the Component Library is done right, while UX (User Experience) Strategy is responsible for doing the main work. Other duties, such as Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis, are consulted to give advice and feedback, and all duties are kept informed about progress and important updates. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, and working together safely and respectfully to reach the Experience Design milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Content Inventory is a list that shows all the information, pictures, and other things on a website or in a project, so you can see what you have, what you need, and what needs to be changed or improved.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Content Inventory and Audit: A Step-by-Step Guide"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/content-inventory/

2. Content Science Review – "How to Do a Content Inventory"  
https://review.content-science.com/how-to-do-a-content-inventory/

3. Moz – "How to Do a Content Audit: Step-by-Step"  
https://moz.com/blog/content-audit
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather all the content from different places, like websites, apps, and documents, and make a list of everything you find. 
2. Work with people who know about the business and design to make sure nothing is missed and to get their advice on what is important. 
3. Organize the content in a clear way, check that it meets the requirements, and keep everyone updated on your progress while making sure everyone feels safe to share their ideas and questions.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Content Inventory helps people doing their duty by showing everything that is already made, so everyone knows what is there, what is missing, and what needs to be fixed or improved, making it easier to make good decisions, stay organized, and work together to reach the goal.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner for a Content Inventory is UX (User Experience) Writing, who is responsible for making sure all the content is listed and organized. UX (User Experience) Writing works closely with other duties like Business Analysis and UX (User Experience) Design, who are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. Product Ownership is accountable along with UX (User Experience) Writing, making sure the Content Inventory meets the requirements and is finished on time. Many other duties, such as Development, Graphic Design, and QA Analysis, are kept informed about the progress. To complete Content Inventories, everyone uses skills like attention to detail, organization, and teamwork, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to make sure the work is done well and everyone feels included.

Deliverable Description: A Content Outline is a list that shows what information needs to be included, how it should be organized, and the order it should go in, so people can easily understand and use it.

1. Gather all the information and topics that need to be included by talking with Business Analysis and UX Design duties. 
2. Organize the information in a clear and logical way, making sure everything is easy to understand and nothing important is missing. 
3. Share the outline with Product Ownership for approval and inform other duties like Development, Graphic Design, and QA Analysis so everyone knows what is planned.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Content Outline is valuable because it helps everyone understand what information needs to be included and how it should be organized, making it easier to make good decisions and take responsibility for the work. By inventorying content and using skills like attention to detail, logical thinking, and communication, a Content Outline supports clear planning and helps people feel safe to share ideas and take ownership, which is important for reaching the milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner for a Content Outline is UX (User Experience) Writing, who is responsible for making sure all the information is clear and organized. UX (User Experience) Writing works closely with other duties like Business Analysis and UX (User Experience) Design, who are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. Product Ownership is accountable, making sure the Content Outlines meet the requirements and are finished on time. Many other duties, such as Development, Graphic Design, and QA Analysis, are informed so they know what is happening. Everyone uses skills like attention to detail, teamwork, and problem-solving, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to help reach important milestones and create a strong Content Outline.

Deliverable Description: A Copywriting Document gives clear instructions for how to write words that help people understand and use a product, using careful thinking, good organization, and strong writing skills, while making sure everyone’s ideas are heard and respected during the process.

It brings together ideas from different duties, like Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Research, so the words used are easy to understand and help people make good decisions. This helps everyone stay organized, work well together, and reach the milestone faster by making sure the information is correct and helpful.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Copywriting Document, the UX (User Experience) Writing duty is responsible for writing clear and helpful content, while Product Ownership and UX (User Experience) Writing are accountable for making sure the Copywriting Document meets the needs and goals. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed about the progress. Deliverable Description: A Customer Experience Diagram shows the steps a customer takes when using a product or service, helping people understand what the customer feels and needs at each stage so they can make things better and easier for everyone.
Online Resources:

Steps to Produce Deliverable:

1. Start by asking the UX Design duty to lead the work and make sure everything is done the right way, while the UX Strategy duty does most of the hands-on work to map out how customers feel and act when using the product. 
2. Get advice and ideas from other duties like UX Research, UX Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis to make the Customer Experience Diagram better.

Deliverable Description: A Database Architecture is a plan that shows how information is organized, stored, and connected in a computer system, and it includes making databases, drawing diagrams to show how data is related, and deciding how everything will work together so people can find and use information easily and safely.
Online Resources:
1. Microsoft Learn – Database Design and Architecture  
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/paths/design-develop-databases/

2. Coursera – Database Systems Concepts & Design (offered by University of Michigan)  
https://www.coursera.org/learn/database-management

3. Vertabelo Blog – Database Modeling and Design Tutorials  
https://vertabelo.com/blog/database-modeling/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. First, the duties of Development and Solutions Architecture work together to create the database by setting up how the data will be stored and organized. 
2. Next, they design how the data connects by making diagrams called Entity Relationship diagrams, which show how different pieces of information relate to each other. 
3. Then, they plan the technical setup by creating the Technical Architecture, which explains how everything will work together. During these steps, Product Ownership, Development, and Solutions Architecture make sure everything is correct and finished on time, while other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, and QA Analysis give advice and share their knowledge. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, communication, and helping others, and follows good practices like making smart choices, taking responsibility, and making sure everyone feels comfortable sharing ideas.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Database Architecture is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand how information is stored, connected, and used, making it easier to build, fix, and improve things together so the group can reach the Architecture milestone faster and with fewer mistakes.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Database Architecture, the duties of Development and Solutions Architecture work together by building the database, designing how the data connects, and planning the technical setup. Product Ownership, Development, and Solutions Architecture are accountable for making sure the Database Architecture is correct and finished on time. Development and Solutions Architecture are responsible for doing the work, like making the database and drawing the diagrams. Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, Information Security Analysis, QA Analysis, Project Management, UX Strategy, and Scrum Master are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed about the progress and results. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follows practices like making good decisions, taking ownership, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas.

Deliverable Description: A Design Annotation gives clear instructions for how something should look and work, so people who build it know exactly what to do. Creating Annotations means writing down details about the design, like where buttons go and what colors to use, to help make good decisions and make sure everyone feels comfortable sharing ideas. Design Annotations help explain what is needed, and they are made by people who study how users think and what they need. Duties include looking at what the business wants, making choices about the product, and learning from users to make the design better.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Annotations in Design Deliverables"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/annotations-in-design-deliverables/

2. Business Analysis and UX (User Experience) Design duties check that the Design Annotations meet all the requirements and are correct, while other duties like UX Research, UX Writing, Product Ownership, and Development give advice and share their knowledge to help improve the annotations.  
3. All duties are kept updated about the progress, and everyone uses good practices like making decisions together, taking responsibility, helping each other, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, and working toward the requirements milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Design Annotation helps everyone understand exactly how something should look and work, so people doing business analysis, product ownership, and UX research can make good decisions, take responsibility, and feel safe sharing ideas. By creating Design Annotations, it’s easier to meet requirements and reach the product milestone because everyone knows what is needed and why.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, UX (User Experience) Design, works with other duties to finish a Design Annotation by making sure the UI specifications are clear for development. Business Analysis and UX (User Experience) Design are accountable, meaning they make sure the Design Annotations meet the requirements and are done right. UX (User Experience) Design is responsible, so this duty actually creates the Design Annotations. Other duties like UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted, which means they give advice and share their knowledge to help make the Design Annotations better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed, so they get updates about the progress. Deliverable Description: An Editorial Style Guide gives clear rules and tips for how to write and design things so everything looks and sounds the same, helping people make good choices about words, pictures, and brand style, while using skills like paying attention, thinking carefully, being creative, and working well with others.
Online Resources:

https://www.nngroup.com/articles/editorial-style-guide/

Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information from different duties like UX Design, UX Research, and Business Analysis to understand the brand’s identity and what users need.
2. The UX Writing duty writes and organizes the Editorial Style Guide, making sure it is clear, easy to use, and matches the brand’s voice.
3. Product Management and UX Writing duties check that the guide is complete and meets all needs, while other duties are kept informed so everyone can use the guide in their work.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Editorial Style Guide is valuable because it helps everyone use the same words, colors, and designs when making things, so the work looks and sounds the same everywhere. This makes it easier to understand what the brand stands for and helps people make good choices when writing or designing. Using an Editorial Style Guide also saves time, helps people work together, and makes sure the brand feels friendly and safe for everyone. This helps reach the goals for requirements and experience design by making everything clear and organized.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create an Editorial Style Guide, the UX (User Experience) Writing duty is responsible for writing and organizing the guide, while Product Management and UX Writing are accountable for making sure it is finished and meets the needs. UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and Product Management are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge about branding, design, and user needs. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others, are informed so everyone knows about the guide and can use it. The process uses skills like attention to detail, creativity, and teamwork, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to make sure the Editorial Style Guide is clear, helpful, and matches the brand’s identity.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: An Empathy Map is a chart that helps you understand what a customer thinks, feels, says, and does by using information from interviews and research, so you can make better decisions and create products that people really want.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Empathy Mapping: The First Step in Design Thinking"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/empathy-mapping/

2. Interaction Design Foundation – "Empathy Map: Complete Guide"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/empathy-map-why-and-how-to-use-it

3. Atlassian Team Playbook – "Empathy Mapping"  
https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook/plays/empathy-mapping
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by doing primary and secondary research to learn about the customer, like talking to people and reading about their needs.  
2. Use customer segmentation to group people with similar needs and gather all the information you find.  
3. The UX Research and Product Management duties lead by organizing the information and making choices, while other duties like Product Ownership, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing give advice and ideas to make the Empathy Map better, and everyone else is kept informed so they can use the Empathy Map in their own work.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Empathy Map helps people doing business analysis, product management, and user experience design understand what customers think, feel, and need, so everyone can make better choices and keep learning about what works best for customers. This makes it easier to spot problems early, share ideas, and create products that really help people, which is important for always improving and reaching the goal of continuous discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create an Empathy Map, the UX (User Experience) Research and Product Management duties are accountable and responsible for making sure the Empathy Map is done right by leading research, organizing information, and making decisions. Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing duties are consulted, which means they give advice and share ideas to help make the Empathy Map better. All other duties, like Development, Graphic Design, QA Analysis, and more, are informed, so they know what is happening and can use the Empathy Map in their own work. Everyone uses skills like collaboration, problem-solving, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the milestone of Continuous Discovery and make sure the Empathy Map helps everyone understand the customer better.

Deliverable Description: An Entity Relationship Diagram is a drawing that shows how different pieces of information in a database are connected, and creating one means you need to think carefully, work with others, and use good problem-solving skills to organize and explain how the data will be stored and used.
Online Resources:
1. Lucidchart ER Diagram Tutorial: https://www.lucidchart.com/pages/er-diagrams
2. Vertabelo Academy ERD Guide: https://academy.vertabelo.com/blog/what-is-an-entity-relationship-diagram/
3. Microsoft Learn - Create ER Diagrams: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/ssms/visual-db-tools/create-a-database-model-entity-relationship-diagram
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. First, the duties of Development and Solutions Architecture work together to build the database and design how the data connects, using problem-solving, logical thinking, and working well with others.  
2. Next, Product Ownership, Development, and Solutions Architecture check that the diagrams are correct and meet what is needed, while Development and Solutions Architecture do the main work of making the diagrams.  
3. Then, duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, Information Security Analysis, QA Analysis, and Project Management give advice and feedback, and everyone is kept up to date about progress and choices, using good decision-making, ownership, and safe, respectful ways to work together to reach the requirements milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Entity Relationship Diagram is valuable because it shows how different pieces of information connect, making it easier for everyone doing their duty to understand, build, and organize a database together, which helps everyone make good decisions and reach the goal faster.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the Entity Relationship Diagrams, the duties of Development and Solutions Architecture work together by building the database and designing how the data connects, using skills like problem-solving, logical thinking, and teamwork. Product Ownership, Development, and Solutions Architecture are accountable for making sure the diagrams are correct and meet the requirements, while Development and Solutions Architecture are responsible for doing the work. Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, Information Security Analysis, QA Analysis, Project Management, Scrum Master, and others are consulted to give advice and feedback, and all other duties are kept informed about progress and decisions. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, and working together safely and respectfully to reach the requirements milestone.

Deliverable Description: Epics and Features are big ideas and important parts of a project that help everyone understand what needs to be built and why, and the duties include creating a clear vision for what should be released, deciding what is most important, listening to feedback, thinking carefully, making smart choices, and working with others to make sure everything is organized and ready for success.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian Agile Coach – Epics, Stories, Themes, and Initiatives  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/epics-stories-themes

2. Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) – Epics and Features  
https://www.scaledagileframework.com/epic/  
https://www.scaledagileframework.com/feature/

3. Scrum.org – How to Split User Stories, Epics, and Features  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/how-split-user-stories-epics-and-features
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by using Release Vision Definition to set a clear goal for what you want to make, with Product Management making sure everything is planned and moving forward, while other duties like UX Design, UX Research, and Development give advice and share ideas.  
2. Next, use Release Scope Definition to decide what features and big ideas (Epics) will be included, making sure everyone understands what needs to be done and when, with Product Management keeping everyone informed about changes and progress.  
3. Throughout the process, everyone works together by making decisions, taking ownership, helping each other, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so the deliverable meets all requirements and important milestones are reached.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Epics and Features help everyone understand what needs to be built and why, so people doing different duties can work together better, make good decisions, and stay focused on what matters most to reach the goal.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For Epics and Features, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible, making sure everything is planned and finished on time by using skills like strategic thinking, problem-solving, and communication. Other duties, such as UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, Business Analysis, and Development, are consulted to give advice and share ideas, while all duties are kept informed about progress and changes. Everyone works together by following practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment, and they use activities like Release Vision Definition and Release Scope Definition to reach important milestones and meet requirements.

2. Ask for advice and information from duties like UX Design, Development, and Product Management to help plan the steps and timing, while keeping all other duties, such as QA Analysis, Graphic Design, and Information Security Analysis, updated about the plan and any changes.  
3. Use skills like organizing, solving problems, and talking clearly, and follow good habits like making decisions, taking responsibility, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so everyone can see the Vision milestone and check how things are going as work moves forward.

This helps everyone stay organized, work together, and feel confident about reaching the goal.

Everyone uses skills like organization, problem-solving, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the Vision milestone and track progress together.

1. Start by having the Product Management duty lead the work, making sure everyone knows what needs to be done and doing most of the tasks like creating the strategy, studying the market, and testing prices. 
2. Ask for advice and ideas from other duties, such as Product Ownership, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Business Analysis, and Development, to make the plan stronger and more useful. 
3. Keep all duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, updated about progress, while everyone uses skills like problem-solving, working together, sharing ideas, and making good decisions, and follows good habits like taking responsibility, being kind when sharing thoughts, and helping each other learn, especially during Continuous Discovery.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Go-To-Market Plan is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand how to launch a product, who the customers are, what price works best, and what steps to take so the product succeeds, making it easier to reach the Continuous Discovery milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Go-To-Market Plan, the Product Management duty is in charge and makes sure everything gets done, while also doing most of the work like developing the strategy, researching the market, and testing prices. Other duties, such as Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Business Analysis, and Development, are asked for their advice and ideas to make the plan better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept up to date about what is happening. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, communication, and decision-making, and follows good practices like taking ownership, making safe spaces to share ideas, and helping each other grow, especially during the important milestone called Continuous Discovery.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: High-Fidelity Prototypes are detailed, interactive models that show exactly how a product will look and work, and creating them means making clickable designs, testing different versions to see which works best, organizing information clearly, and quickly coming up with new ideas, all while using careful thinking, creativity, and good communication to make sure the final product is easy to use and understand.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – High-Fidelity Prototypes: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/high-fidelity-prototype/
2. Adobe XD Ideas – How to Create High-Fidelity Prototypes: https://xd.adobe.com/ideas/process/prototyping/high-fidelity-prototype/
3. Figma Learn – Prototyping in Figma: https://help.figma.com/hc/en-us/articles/360040451373-Create-prototypes-in-Figma
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by quickly coming up with lots of ideas and organizing them to make sure the information is easy to find and use. 
2. Create a clickable prototype that looks and works like the real thing, using design tools and making sure it matches the goals. 
3. Test different versions with users to see which one works best, get advice and feedback from other duties, and make changes until the prototype is ready and everyone is informed about the progress.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: High-Fidelity Prototypes help people working together by showing exactly how something will look and work before it is finished, so everyone can give feedback, make better choices, and fix problems early, which makes it easier to reach the Experience Design milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create High-Fidelity Prototypes, the duty owner, which is UX (User Experience) Design, works closely with other duties like UX (User Experience) Research, Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Strategy, and UX (User Experience) Writing by sharing ideas, testing designs, and making sure everything fits together. The duties that are accountable make sure the High-Fidelity Prototypes meet the goals and are finished on time, while the responsible duties actually do the work like making clickable prototypes and testing them. Consulted duties give advice and feedback to help improve the prototypes, and informed duties are kept up to date about progress and important changes. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the Experience Design milestone together.
