Deliverable Description: A Hosting Infrastructure gives clear instructions for creating, organizing, and managing the computer systems and networks that help websites and apps run smoothly, using careful planning, problem-solving, and good communication to make sure everything works safely and reliably.
Online Resources:
1. Microsoft Learn – Host and deploy web applications  
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/paths/host-deploy-web-applications/

2. AWS Training and Certification – Getting Started with AWS Hosting  
https://aws.amazon.com/training/learning-paths/cloud-practitioner/

3. DigitalOcean Community Tutorials – Hosting Infrastructure  
https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The Solutions Architecture duty leads by making important choices and building the technical plans for the Hosting Infrastructure, making sure the design is strong and fits the needs. 
2. Product Ownership and Solutions Architecture check that everything is done correctly, while other duties like UX Design, Business Analysis, and Product Management give advice and share their knowledge to help. 
3. Everyone keeps each other updated, uses skills like problem-solving, communication, and working together, and follows good practices like making smart decisions, taking responsibility, and supporting each other to reach the architecture milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Hosting Infrastructure helps everyone working together by making sure all the parts of a project can run safely and smoothly online, which is important for reaching the Architecture milestone. When people use skills like problem-solving, communication, and organization to create a strong technical plan, it lets everyone know how things will work and keeps everything connected. This makes it easier to make good decisions, take responsibility, and help each other, so the project can move forward without problems.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Hosting Infrastructure, the Solutions Architecture duty leads the work by making important decisions and building the technical plans, while Product Ownership and Solutions Architecture are accountable for making sure everything is done right. Solutions Architecture is responsible for doing the main activities, like creating the technical architecture. Other duties, such as UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Business Analysis, Product Management, and Scrum Master, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follows practices like making good decisions, taking ownership, and helping each other feel safe and empowered to reach the architecture milestone for the Hosting Infrastructure.

Deliverable Description: A How Might We (HMW) Statement is a clear question that helps people think about how to solve a problem, and making these statements means you need to pay close attention, think carefully, and use good communication to turn a problem into a helpful question that guides everyone toward finding solutions.
Online Resources:
1. IDEO Design Kit – How Might We  
https://www.designkit.org/methods/how-might-we

2. Interaction Design Foundation – How to Frame Your Design Challenge with HMW Questions  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/how-to-frame-your-design-challenge-with-how-might-we-statements

3. Nielsen Norman Group – How Might We Questions  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/how-might-we-questions/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by using critical thinking and problem-solving to look at the problem and write clear problem statements.  
2. Get advice and ideas from Product Management, UX Research, and UX Strategy duties to make the statements better and more helpful.  
3. Use good communication, decision-making, and ownership to finish the How Might We (HMW) Statements, making sure everyone feels safe to share and the statements are clear and ready to help solve problems.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A How Might We (HMW) Statements helps people doing different duties work together by turning problems into clear questions everyone can solve, making it easier to share ideas, stay focused, and keep finding better ways to reach the goal of continuous discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the How Might We (HMW) Statements, the Business Analysis duty is responsible for generating problem statements using skills like critical thinking, problem-solving, and clear communication, while Product Ownership is accountable for making sure the statements are completed well. 2. Product Operations keeps everything organized and checks that all duties, like Agile Coaching, Product Ownership, UX Design, and Development, know what is happening and what is expected, asking for advice and sharing updates.  
3. Everyone uses good habits like making decisions together, taking responsibility, helping each other, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, and giving each other the power to do their best, so the Kanban Board Statuses are clear and help reach the intake milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: The Kanban Board Statuses are valuable because they help everyone see what work is being done, what is finished, and what still needs to be started, making it easier to organize duties, make good decisions, and keep everyone on the same page so the group can reach the Intake milestone together.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For the Kanban Board Statuses, the Product Operations duty owner works with all other duties by making sure everyone knows what to do and when to do it, using skills like meeting facilitation, negotiation, and problem-solving. Product Operations is responsible for keeping things organized and moving forward, while the Scrum Master and Product Operations are accountable for making sure everything is done right. Duties like Agile Coaching, Product Ownership, UX Design, Development, and others are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, and all duties are kept informed about what is happening. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to help each other, reach the intake milestone, and make the Kanban Board Statuses successful.

Deliverable Description: The Key Performance Indicators (KPI's) are a set of clear goals that help you measure how well you are doing your duty, like starting a project by leading a kickoff meeting, making sure everyone understands what needs to be done, listening to others, solving problems, making smart choices, and keeping everything organized so the project can be successful from the very beginning.

2. Use hard skills like organizing information, asking good questions, and paying close attention to details, while practicing good habits like making decisions together, being responsible, helping others, making everyone feel safe to share ideas, and always trying to get better. 
3. Each duty gives advice and shares their knowledge, while Business Analysis makes sure everything is done correctly and Project Management checks the final results, so that the KPI's are clear, useful, and ready to help everyone understand how things are going.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Key Performance Indicators (KPI's) are valuable because they help everyone understand what success looks like and what needs to be done to reach the goal during the Intake milestone. By using Key Performance Indicators (KPI's), people can track progress, make better decisions, and stay focused on what matters most, which makes it easier to work together and achieve the right results.

Deliverable Description: Logo and Brand Assets are the pictures, symbols, and designs that show what a company or group is all about, and making them means coming up with ideas, drawing, and choosing the best look to help people remember and trust the company.
Online Resources:
1. Adobe Creative Cloud Blog – "How to Deliver Brand Assets to Clients": https://blog.adobe.com/en/publish/2021/06/15/how-to-deliver-brand-assets-to-clients

Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by forming the brand identity, which means deciding what the brand should look and feel like, using creativity and problem-solving to make sure it matches the goals. 
2. Next, create illustrations, icons, and logos by drawing and designing images and words that fit the brand, paying close attention to details and making sure everything looks good together. 
3. Throughout the process, duties like UX Writing and Graphic Design make the designs and check that the words and pictures fit the brand, while Product Management and others give advice, feedback, and make sure everything is finished and meets the goals, using good decision-making, ownership, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Logo and Brand Assets are valuable because they help everyone doing their duty understand and share the same look and message, making it easier to create a great experience for people using the product. This helps reach the Experience Design milestone by making sure all the pictures, colors, and words match, so everything feels connected and clear.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the Logo and Brand Assets, the duties of UX (User Experience) Writing and Graphic Design are responsible for making the designs and making sure the words and pictures fit the brand, while Product Management, UX (User Experience) Writing, and Graphic Design are accountable for making sure everything is finished and meets the goals. Other duties like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, Product Ownership, Product Management, and Account Management are consulted to give advice and feedback on the designs and ideas. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Agile Coaching, and others, are kept informed about the progress and results. The main activities are Brand Identity Formation, Creating Illustrations, and Icon and Logo Creation, and everyone uses skills like creativity, attention to detail, problem-solving, and teamwork, following practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the Experience Design milestone.

Deliverable Description: Low-Fidelity Prototypes are simple, early versions of a product that help you show your ideas, test different designs, and organize information before making the real thing, using skills like problem-solving, creativity, and communication while making sure everyone feels safe to share their thoughts and make decisions together.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Low-Fidelity Prototyping"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/low-fidelity-prototyping/

2. Interaction Design Foundation – "Low-Fidelity Prototyping: Creating Fast, Effective, and Usable Designs"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/low-fidelity-prototyping-creating-fast-effective-and-usable-designs

3. Adobe XD Ideas – "How to Create Low-Fidelity Wireframes and Prototypes"  
https://xd.adobe.com/ideas/process/wireframing/low-fidelity-wireframes-prototypes/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by quickly sketching out ideas and building simple models to show how the product will work, using paper or basic digital tools. 
2. Work together with other duties to make clickable prototypes, test different versions, and organize the information so it is easy to use and understand. 
3. Share the prototypes with consulted duties for feedback, make changes based on their advice, and keep everyone informed about progress, using good communication, creativity, and problem-solving to make sure the prototypes meet the goals and are finished on time.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Low-Fidelity Prototypes are valuable because they help people with different duties quickly share ideas, test out what works best, and fix problems early, so everyone can agree on how something should look and work before spending a lot of time and effort building it for real, which helps everyone reach the Experience Design milestone faster and with fewer mistakes.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create Low-Fidelity Prototypes, the duty owner, which is UX (User Experience) Design, works closely with other duties like UX (User Experience) Research, Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Strategy, and UX (User Experience) Writing. The duties that are accountable make sure the Low-Fidelity Prototypes meet the goals and are finished on time, while the responsible duties do the main work, such as making clickable prototypes, testing ideas, and building the information structure. Consulted duties give advice and share their knowledge to help improve the prototypes, and informed duties are kept up to date about progress and important decisions. All these duties use skills like problem-solving, teamwork, creativity, and communication, and they follow practices like decision-making, ownership, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so the Low-Fidelity Prototypes are useful and easy to understand.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Market Research Analysis is a report that explains what people want, what problems they have, and what products or services they might buy, using facts and data to help make smart choices about what to create or improve.
Online Resources:
1. HubSpot Blog – Market Research: The Complete Guide  
https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/market-research

2. SurveyMonkey – Market Research: The Ultimate Guide  
https://www.surveymonkey.com/mp/market-research/

3. Coursera – Market Research Specialization by University of California, Davis  
https://www.coursera.org/specializations/market-research
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Product Management leads by planning what information is needed about the market and asks for help from other duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, and Business Analysis, who share their ideas and knowledge. 
2. Everyone works together by collecting facts, talking to people, looking at data, and writing down what they find, while using skills like paying attention to details, thinking carefully, and solving problems. 
3. Product Management puts all the information together, checks it for accuracy, and shares updates with all duties, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas and keeps learning new things so the Market Research Analysis stays helpful and up to date.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Market Research Analysis helps everyone working on a duty understand what customers want and what problems they have, so people can make better choices and create things that really help others. By using a Market Research Analysis, people can find out what is working, what needs to change, and how to keep improving, which is important for always learning and making things better together.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete a Market Research Analysis, the Product Management duty is in charge and makes sure everything gets done right, while also doing most of the work. Product Management asks for help and ideas from duties like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Product Ownership, Business Analysis, and Development, who share their knowledge and advice. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept updated about what is happening. Everyone uses skills like paying attention to details, thinking carefully, working together, and solving problems, and they follow good habits like making smart choices, taking responsibility, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. The main goal is to keep learning new things about the market all the time, so the Market Research Analysis is always useful and up to date.

Deliverable Description: A Marketing Plan is a guide that explains how to introduce a product to customers, including steps like researching the market, deciding who the customers are, figuring out the best way to talk about the product, and planning how to sell it, while using skills like problem-solving, communication, and organization.
Online Resources:
1. HubSpot Academy – How to Create a Marketing Plan: https://academy.hubspot.com/courses/how-to-create-a-marketing-plan  
2. Hootsuite Blog – How to Create a Marketing Plan: https://blog.hootsuite.com/how-to-create-a-marketing-plan/  
3. American Marketing Association – Marketing Plan Tutorials: https://www.ama.org/marketing-news/marketing-plan-template/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by doing market research to learn about customers and what they need. 
2. Use the information to divide customers into groups and decide how to talk about the product so it stands out (product positioning and customer segmentation). 
3. Create a go-to-market strategy that explains how to reach customers and share the product with them. The Product Management duty makes sure each step is done well, using skills like thinking carefully, working together, and talking clearly. Other duties, like Product Ownership, UX Design, UX Research, and Business Analysis, give advice and share their knowledge, while everyone else is kept up to date on progress and important choices. All duties share ideas, make decisions together, and use good practices like taking responsibility, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to speak up, so the Marketing Plan matches the vision milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Marketing Plan is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand who the product is for, how to talk about it, and what steps to take to reach customers, making it easier to work together and move closer to the vision milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Marketing Plan, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible for making sure all the steps are done right, like developing the go-to-market strategy, product positioning, customer segmentation, and market research, using skills such as critical thinking, teamwork, and communication. Other duties, like Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Business Analysis, and Development, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, while all duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress and important decisions. Everyone works together by sharing ideas, making decisions, and using practices like ownership, service leadership, and psychological safety to reach the vision milestone for the Marketing Plan.

Deliverable Description: A Meeting Agenda is a list that shows what will be talked about and done during a meeting, helping everyone stay organized, make good decisions, and use time wisely by following the plan and making sure all important topics are covered.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – "How to Design an Agenda for an Effective Meeting"  
https://hbr.org/2015/03/how-to-design-an-agenda-for-an-effective-meeting

2. MindTools – "Running Effective Meetings: Establishing an Agenda"  
https://www.mindtools.com/commtsk/RunningMeetings.htm

3. Atlassian Team Playbook – "Meeting Agenda Template & Guide"  
https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook/plays/meeting-agenda
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Plan when the meeting will happen and make sure everyone knows the time. 
2. Write down what topics need to be talked about and what decisions need to be made. 
3. Share the agenda with everyone before the meeting so they can get ready. 

To make a good Meeting Agenda, the duty of Project Management is to organize and make sure all the steps, like scheduling and setting what will be talked about, are done on time and correctly by using skills like being organized, talking clearly, and solving problems. Product Management checks that the agenda matches all the important project steps and is done the right way. All duties give advice and ideas to help make the agenda better, and everyone, like Business Analysis, Development, and Graphic Design, is kept up to date. Everyone works together by making good choices, taking responsibility, helping each other, making sure everyone feels safe to share, and letting everyone help, so the Meeting Agenda is clear, useful, and helps the project move forward.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Meeting Agendas are valuable because they help everyone know what will be talked about, keep the group organized, and make sure important topics are covered so the group can reach the milestone together.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: Project Management is the duty owner for Meeting Agendas and is responsible for making sure all the activities like scheduling meetings and setting expectations are done on time and correctly, using skills like organization, communication, and problem-solving. Product Management is accountable, meaning they make sure Meeting Agendas meet all product milestones and are done right. All duties are consulted, so they give advice and share ideas to help make Meeting Agendas better, while everyone—including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others—is kept informed about what is happening. Everyone works together by practicing good decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to make sure Meeting Agendas are clear, helpful, and support all the important steps in the project.

Deliverable Description: Microcopy gives short, clear instructions or helpful hints in apps or websites, making it easier for people to know what to do next or understand what is happening, and creating a friendly and simple experience for everyone.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – Microcopy: How to Write Small UI Text That Gets Noticed  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/microcopy/

2. UX Collective – Microcopy: How to Write Small Texts That Make a Big Impact  
https://uxdesign.cc/microcopy-how-to-write-small-texts-that-make-a-big-impact-6c3f7c6c4e3e

3. Content Design London – Microcopy: What it is and how to write it  
https://contentdesign.london/blog/microcopy-what-it-is-and-how-to-write-it/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The UX Writing duty writes clear and helpful words that people will see and use in the product, making sure every message is easy to understand and friendly. 
2. The Product Ownership and UX Writing duties check that the words are correct and finished on time, while other duties like UX Design, UX Research, and Business Analysis give advice and ideas to make the words even better. 
3. All duties share updates and information, make choices together, and show leadership so the Microcopy is ready for the Experience Design milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Microcopies are valuable because they help everyone understand how to use a product easily and clearly, making sure people don’t get confused or stuck. When people who do product ownership, UX design, and UX research work together to write microcopies, they use their skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, and communication to make the experience better for everyone. This helps reach the experience design milestone by making sure the product is simple, helpful, and enjoyable for all users.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner for Microcopy is UX (User Experience) Writing, which means this duty is responsible for writing the words people see and use in a product. Product Ownership and UX (User Experience) Writing are both accountable, making sure the Microcopies are correct and finished on time. UX (User Experience) Writing is responsible for doing the copywriting work, using skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, and good communication. UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and Product Management are consulted, so they give advice and share ideas to help make the Microcopies better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others, are informed, so they know what is happening with the Microcopies. Everyone works together by sharing information, making decisions, and showing leadership to reach the Experience Design milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Microservices Architecture is a way of designing computer programs by breaking them into smaller parts that each do one job, so it is easier to build, fix, and change things. The duty is to create a clear plan for how these parts will work together, using careful thinking, good communication, and smart choices. This includes writing down how each part connects, making sure everyone understands, and always looking for ways to make things better.
Online Resources:
1. Microsoft Learn – Microservices Architecture: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/microservices/
2. AWS Microservices Guide: https://aws.amazon.com/microservices/
3. Spring.io – Building Microservices with Spring Boot: https://spring.io/guides/gs/spring-boot-docker/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The Solutions Architecture duty leads by making a clear and complete technical plan for how the microservices will work together. 
2. Product Ownership and Solutions Architecture check and approve the plan, while Business Analysis, Product Management, Scrum Master, and UX duties give helpful advice and share what they know to make smart choices. 
3. All duties, like Development and QA Analysis, stay updated about progress and important decisions, using good problem-solving, communication, and working together, while following practices like taking responsibility, making decisions, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Microservices Architecture helps people with different duties work together by breaking a big system into smaller parts that are easier to build, fix, and understand. This makes it simpler to organize work, solve problems, and make good decisions, so everyone can reach the Architecture milestone faster and with less confusion.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Microservices Architecture, the Solutions Architecture duty leads the work and is responsible for making sure the technical plans are correct and complete, while Product Ownership and Solutions Architecture are accountable for the final result. Business Analysis, Product Management, Scrum Master, and the UX duties are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, helping to make good decisions. All duties, including Development, QA Analysis, and others, are kept informed about progress and important choices. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follows practices like ownership, decision-making, and psychological safety to reach the architecture milestone together.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Network Architecture is a plan that shows how computers and devices connect and talk to each other safely, including setting up firewalls, checking for security problems, testing for weaknesses, creating rules to protect information, building the technical setup, and finding possible threats, all while using careful thinking, good communication, and strong organization skills.
Online Resources:
1. Cisco Networking Academy – https://www.netacad.com/courses/networking/network-engineer
2. Microsoft Learn: Network Architecture – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/training/paths/architect-network-infrastructure/
3. Pluralsight: Network Architecture Courses – https://www.pluralsight.com/paths/network-architecture
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by working with other duties to gather all the needs and rules for the network, using skills like problem-solving and clear communication. 
2. Set up the technical plan by creating the network’s structure, defining security protocols, and making sure the firewall is configured to protect information. 
3. Check the network’s safety by doing information security audits, threat modeling, and penetration testing, while sharing updates and getting advice from other duties to make sure the network is safe, works well, and meets all requirements.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Network Architecture helps everyone doing their duty understand how computers and devices connect and stay safe, so they can work together better and protect important information. By setting up firewalls, checking for security problems, and planning how everything fits together, Network Architectures make sure the system is strong and ready for anything, which helps reach the Architecture milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Network Architecture, the Information Security Analysis duty owner works with other duties like Solutions Architecture, Business Analysis, Development, Product Ownership, and Product Management by sharing skills such as problem-solving, logical thinking, and teamwork. Information Security Analysis is responsible for activities like firewall configuration, security audits, and threat modeling, while Product Management and Information Security Analysis are accountable for making sure the Network Architecture meets all requirements. Duties like Product Ownership, Business Analysis, Development, QA Analysis, and UX Design are consulted to give advice and feedback, and all other duties are kept informed about progress and important decisions. Everyone uses good practices like decision-making, ownership, and clear communication to reach the Architecture milestone together.

Deliverable Description: An Objectives and Key Results (OKR's) is a clear list of important goals and the steps to reach them, and your duty is to help start the process by leading a kickoff meeting, asking good questions, making sure everyone understands what needs to be done, and using skills like problem-solving, communication, and organization to keep everything on track.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – "Measure What Matters: How Google, Bono, and the Gates Foundation Rock the World with OKRs"  
https://hbr.org/2018/05/the-okrs-field-book

2. Google re:Work – Guide: Set goals with OKRs  
https://rework.withgoogle.com/guides/set-goals-with-okrs/steps/introduction/

3. Atlassian – OKR examples and how to write them  
https://www.atlassian.com/blog/productivity/how-to-write-okrs-examples
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by having a kickoff meeting where the Business Analysis duty leads everyone to talk about what the group wants to achieve and what results will show success.  
2. Ask for advice from duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture to make sure the goals and results are clear and possible.  
3. Keep all duties, such as Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, updated about the progress, using skills like leading meetings, paying attention to details, and working well with others, while making decisions together and helping each other grow.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: An Objectives and Key Results (OKR's) helps everyone doing their duty understand what is most important to reach during a project, so people can focus on the same goals and measure progress together, making it easier to reach the intake milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete the Objectives and Key Results (OKR's), the Business Analysis duty is responsible for making sure all the right steps are followed, while Project Management is accountable for the final outcome. Other duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about what is happening. Everyone works together by using skills like meeting facilitation, negotiation, attention to detail, and teamwork, and by following practices such as decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, empowerment, agility, and continuous growth, especially during the Intake milestone.

Deliverable Description: A Personal Growth Roadmap gives clear steps and helpful advice for doing duties like coaching others, solving problems, making good decisions, and working well with others, so you can keep learning, handle challenges, and become better at your duties over time.
Online Resources:
1. MindTools – Personal Development Planning: https://www.mindtools.com/a4wo118/personal-development-plan

Steps to Produce Deliverable:

1. Start by having the Agile Coaching duty lead the work, making sure everyone knows what to do and helping others learn and grow through coaching.  
2. Ask for advice and ideas from other duties like Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and Product Management to make the Personal Growth Roadmap better, and keep everyone updated on progress.  
3. Use good skills like problem-solving, communication, and decision-making, and follow helpful practices like being open, supporting each other, and always trying to get better, making sure the Personal Growth Roadmap helps everyone learn and reach all the important milestones.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Personal Growth Roadmap is valuable because it helps everyone doing different duties see how they can get better at their work, learn new skills, and support each other. By following a Personal Growth Roadmap, people can set clear goals, track their progress, and work together to solve problems, which makes it easier to reach important milestones and keep growing as a group.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Personal Growth Roadmap, the Agile Coaching duty is in charge and makes sure everything gets done, while also doing most of the work like coaching others and helping them grow. Other duties such as Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and Product Management are asked for advice and ideas to make the Personal Growth Roadmaps better, and they are also kept up to date on how things are going. Everyone works together by using skills like problem-solving, communication, and decision-making, and they follow good practices like being open, helping each other, and always trying to improve. Important steps include starting the work and reaching all the product milestones, and everyone is responsible for making sure the Personal Growth Roadmaps help people learn and grow.

Deliverable Description: A Pitch Deck is a set of slides that explains a product’s vision, shows how it will be sold to customers, describes who the customers are, shares research about the market, and tells the plan for launching the product, using clear thinking, good communication, and smart decision-making.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – "How to Create a Winning Pitch Deck"  
https://hbr.org/2022/03/how-to-create-a-winning-pitch-deck

2. Y Combinator – "How to Pitch Your Startup"  
https://www.ycombinator.com/library/6h-how-to-pitch-your-startup

3. Slidebean – "How to Present a Pitch Deck" (Video Tutorial)  
https://slidebean.com/blog/startups-how-to-present-a-pitch-deck
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by doing market research to learn about customers and what they need, then use this information to decide who the product is for and how it should be shown to them.  
2. Work together with different duties, like Product Ownership, UX Design, UX Research, and Business Analysis, to create a go-to-market strategy, position the product, and define the vision for the release, making sure everyone shares ideas and gives advice.  
3. Use good practices like making decisions together, taking responsibility, helping each other, and making sure everyone feels safe to share, so the Pitch Deck is clear, easy to understand, and shows the product’s vision in the best way.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Pitch Deck is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand the big idea, the plan to reach customers, and what makes the product special, so everyone can work together to reach the Vision milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Pitch Deck, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible for making sure all the work gets done, like developing the go-to-market strategy, product positioning, customer segmentation, market research, and defining the release vision, using skills such as critical thinking, teamwork, and communication. Product Management works with other duties, like Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, and Development, who are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress and important decisions. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to make sure the Pitch Decks are clear, helpful, and ready to share the vision.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Positioning Statement is a clear message that explains what a product is, who it is for, and why it is special, using research about customers and the market, and it helps guide decisions about how to sell and talk about the product.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – "How to Develop a Positioning Statement"  
https://hbr.org/2013/04/a-refresher-on-positioning

2. HubSpot – "How to Write a Positioning Statement"  
https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/positioning-statement

3. MindTools – "Positioning: Creating and Communicating a Competitive Advantage"  
https://www.mindtools.com/a4wo118/positioning
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by doing market research and customer segmentation to learn about the people who might use the product and what they need. 
2. Work with other duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, and Business Analysis to gather ideas and information, using skills like critical thinking, communication, and decision-making. 
3. Use all the information to write a clear Positioning Statement that explains what makes the product special and why people should want it, while keeping everyone informed about the progress, especially during the Continuous Discovery milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Positioning Statement is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand exactly who the product is for, what makes it special, and why people should care, so all the different duties can work together and make smart choices as they keep learning and improving the product.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: A Product Management duty owner works with other duties like Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, and Development to create a Positioning Statement by using skills such as critical thinking, teamwork, and communication, and by following practices like decision-making and ownership. Product Management is accountable and responsible for making sure the Positioning Statement is done right, while duties like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Product Ownership, Business Analysis, and Development are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress and results, especially during the Continuous Discovery milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: The Prioritized Backlog Tasks are a list of important things that need to be done first, and they help everyone know what to work on next by showing what is most important and why, using careful thinking, good communication, and smart choices to keep everything organized and moving forward.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org - Scrum Guide and Resources  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/scrum-guide

2. Atlassian Agile Coach - Prioritizing the Backlog  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/product-management/prioritization

3. Mountain Goat Software - Product Backlog Example  
https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/agile/scrum/product-backlog
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Write down all the tasks that need to be done and make sure each task is clear and easy to understand.  
2. Talk with everyone doing a duty to figure out which tasks are most important, using good listening, sharing ideas, and making smart choices together.  
3. Put the most important tasks at the top of the list, keep everyone updated about any changes, and make sure everyone feels safe to share their thoughts so the right tasks get done first and the requirements are met.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Prioritized Backlog Tasks list is valuable because it helps everyone know which duties are most important to work on first, making it easier to stay organized, make good decisions, and reach the requirements milestone faster.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: Product Ownership is responsible for making sure the Prioritized Backlog Tasks are clear and organized, while Product Management is accountable for the final results. All duties work together by sharing ideas and giving feedback, using skills like problem-solving, communication, and decision-making. Everyone is consulted to help identify what is most important, and all duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others, are kept informed about changes and progress. By practicing ownership, service leadership, and psychological safety, each duty helps move the Prioritized Backlog Tasks forward, making sure requirements are met and everyone feels empowered to contribute.

Deliverable Description: Problem Statements are clear descriptions of a problem that needs to be solved, created by gathering information, thinking carefully, and talking with others to make sure everyone understands what is needed and why it matters.
Online Resources:
1. IDEO U – "How to Write a Problem Statement"  
https://www.ideou.com/blogs/inspiration/how-might-we

2. Interaction Design Foundation – "How to Define a Problem Statement"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/how-to-define-a-problem-statement

3. Harvard Business Review – "Are You Solving the Right Problem?"  
https://hbr.org/2017/01/are-you-solving-the-right-problem
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Gather information by talking with everyone who has ideas or needs, asking questions, and listening carefully to understand what problems need to be solved.  
2. Work together with other duties like Product Management, Product Ownership, and UX Research to write clear and complete Problem Statements, making sure everyone’s thoughts are included and the statements are easy to understand.  
3. Share the Problem Statements with all duties, get advice from experts like UX Design and Solutions Architecture, and keep everyone updated, using good communication, problem-solving, and decision-making skills while making sure everyone feels safe and included.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Problem Statements are valuable because they help everyone understand exactly what problem needs to be solved, making it easier for people with different duties to work together, share ideas, and make good decisions so the group can reach the requirements milestone faster and with less confusion.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the Problem Statements, the Business Analysis duty owner works with other duties like Product Management, Product Ownership, and UX (User Experience) Research by gathering requirements, asking questions, and making sure everyone’s ideas are heard. Business Analysis is accountable and responsible for making sure the Problem Statements are clear and complete, while Product Ownership also shares accountability. Duties like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Writing, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, communication, and decision-making, and follows practices like ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the requirements milestone for the Problem Statements.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Product-Market Fit Analysis is a report that shows if a product matches what customers want by using research, customer groups, and smart planning, and it helps people make good choices about how to sell and improve the product.
Online Resources:
1. Y Combinator - "How to Find Product Market Fit"  
https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-how-to-find-product-market-fit

2. First Round Review - "The Product-Market Fit Pyramid: A Framework to Guide Your Growth"  
https://review.firstround.com/the-product-market-fit-pyramid-a-framework-to-guide-your-growth

3. Harvard Business Review - "Achieving Product-Market Fit"  
https://hbr.org/2022/05/achieving-product-market-fit
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by doing market research to learn what customers want and what problems they have.  
2. Use customer segmentation to group customers by their needs and interests, then work on product positioning to show how the product helps each group.  
3. Create a go-to-market strategy to plan how to share the product with customers and keep checking if the product fits what customers want by asking for feedback and making changes.  
Product Management makes sure all the work gets done, while other duties like Product Ownership, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, and Business Analysis give ideas and feedback to help make smart choices and solve problems. Everyone uses skills like thinking carefully, working together, talking clearly, and making good decisions, and follows practices like taking responsibility, helping others, feeling safe to share ideas, and feeling empowered to keep learning and improving the Product-Market Fit Analysis.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Product-Market Fit Analysis helps everyone doing their duty understand if what they are making is something people really want and need, so they can keep improving it and make better choices together during Continuous Discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For a Product-Market Fit Analysis, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible for making sure all the work gets done, like creating a go-to-market strategy, product positioning, customer segmentation, and market research. Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, and Development duties are consulted to give their ideas and feedback, helping to make smart decisions and solve problems. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about what is happening and the progress being made. Everyone uses skills like critical thinking, teamwork, communication, and decision-making, and follows practices like ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the milestone of continuous discovery and make sure the Product-Market Fit Analysis is successful.
Required Activities: Customer Segmentation,Developing Go-To-Market Strategy,Market Research,Product Positioning

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Project Goals Definition is a clear list of what needs to be done, why it matters, and how everyone will work together to reach the goals, created by talking with people, asking questions, and making sure everyone understands and agrees on what success looks like.
Online Resources:
1. Project Management Institute (PMI) – "How to Define Project Goals and Objectives"  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/defining-project-goals-objectives-6896

2. Wrike Blog – "How to Define Project Goals and Objectives"  
https://www.wrike.com/blog/how-to-define-project-goals-objectives/

3. Smartsheet – "How to Write SMART Project Objectives"  
https://www.smartsheet.com/how-write-smart-project-objectives
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by having a kickoff meeting where the Business Analysis duty leads everyone through what the project is about and what needs to be done.  
2. Ask for advice and information from other duties like UX Design, Product Management, and Development, making sure everyone shares what they know and helps make the project goals clear.  
3. Use skills like talking in front of others, paying close attention, and working well together, while following good habits like making decisions together, being responsible, helping each other, feeling safe to share ideas, and always trying to get better, so that everyone understands and agrees on the project goals before moving forward.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Project Goals Definition is valuable because it helps everyone understand what needs to be done, why it matters, and how to work together to reach the goal, making it easier to stay organized, make good decisions, and solve problems during the Intake milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete a Project Goals Definition, the Business Analysis duty leads the work and is responsible for making sure all the right steps happen, while Project Management is accountable for the final result. Other duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about what is happening. Everyone uses skills like meeting facilitation, negotiation, public speaking, attention to detail, and teamwork, and follows practices such as decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, empowerment, and continuous growth to reach the Intake milestone and make sure the Project Goals Definition is clear and successful.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Project Hand-off is when you give clear instructions and all the important information to the next person or group so they can continue the work smoothly, making sure everyone understands what needs to be done, what has already been finished, and what problems might come up, while using good communication, careful planning, and strong decision-making skills.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian: "Project handover checklist"  
https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook/plays/project-handover

2. Project Management Institute (PMI): "Project Handover: Best Practices"  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/project-handover-best-practices-11013

3. Wrike Blog: "How to Create a Project Handover Document"  
https://www.wrike.com/blog/how-to-create-a-project-handover-document/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start with a kickoff or intake meeting, where the Business Analysis duty leads everyone to understand what needs to be done and makes sure all duties know their part. 
2. Use hard skills like meeting facilitation, problem-solving, and clear communication to gather advice from duties like UX Design, Development, and Product Management, and keep everyone updated on progress. 
3. Follow good practices such as making decisions together, taking responsibility, helping each other, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, and always trying to get better, so the Project Hand-off is ready for launch and everyone knows what to do next.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Project Hand-off is valuable because it helps everyone understand what needs to be done, who is responsible for each duty, and how to work together to reach the Launch milestone. It makes sure all important information is shared, questions are answered, and everyone feels ready and confident to move forward. This helps avoid confusion, keeps things organized, and makes it easier to solve problems and make good decisions along the way.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For a Project Hand-off, the Business Analysis duty works with all other duties by leading the activities like kickoff and intake, using skills such as meeting facilitation, problem-solving, and communication. Project Management is accountable for making sure the Project Hand-off is done right, while Business Analysis is responsible for carrying out the work. Duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, and Account Management, are kept informed about the progress. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, empowerment, and continuous growth to reach the launch milestone and make sure the Project Hand-off is successful.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Project Plan is a set of clear instructions that explains how to start a project, what risks to look out for, and how to organize all the steps needed to finish the project successfully, using careful thinking, good communication, and strong decision-making skills.
Online Resources:
1. Project Management Institute (PMI) – "How to Create a Project Plan"  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/how-to-create-project-plan-11029

2. Wrike Blog – "How to Create a Project Plan in 5 Steps"  
https://www.wrike.com/blog/how-to-create-a-project-plan/

3. Smartsheet – "How to Write a Project Plan"  
https://www.smartsheet.com/how-write-project-plan
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Gather all the important information about the project and talk with everyone who has knowledge to share, making sure to listen and ask questions.  
2. Look for any risks or problems that could happen and write them down, so everyone knows what to watch out for and can help find solutions.  
3. Start the project with a kickoff meeting, where everyone learns what the project is about, what needs to be done, and how to work together, using good communication, problem-solving, and making sure everyone feels included and safe.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Project Plan is valuable because it helps everyone understand what needs to be done, when it needs to happen, and who is responsible, making it easier to spot risks early and keep all duties organized so the group can reach the Intake milestone smoothly.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete a Project Plan, the duty owners, Project Management and Business Analysis, work together by organizing and checking all the details, making sure risks are understood, and starting the project with a kickoff. They are accountable for making sure the Project Plan is finished and correct. Project Management and Business Analysis are responsible for doing the work, like gathering information and making decisions. Other duties, such as Product Ownership, Scrum Master, UX Design, Development, and more, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others, are kept informed about the progress. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, communication, and teamwork, and follows practices like making good decisions, showing leadership, and helping each other feel safe and included, especially during the Intake milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: Proto Personas are simple sketches or descriptions of different types of customers that help you understand who might use your product, what they need, and how they think, so you can make better decisions and create things that work well for them.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Proto-Personas: A Simple Tool to Guide Product Development"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/proto-personas/

2. Interaction Design Foundation – "How to Create Proto-Personas to Guide Design"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/how-to-create-proto-personas-to-guide-design

3. UX Mastery – "How to Create a Persona"  
https://uxmastery.com/how-to-create-a-persona/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information about different types of customers by doing customer segmentation, which means sorting people into groups based on what they need or like.
2. Product Management makes sure everything gets done and makes the final decisions, while Product Ownership and UX Research share their ideas and knowledge to help create the Proto Personas.
3. Keep everyone else, like Business Analysis, Development, and Graphic Design, updated on what is happening, and use skills like paying attention to details, working together, solving problems, making decisions, and making sure everyone feels safe to share their thoughts, so the Proto Personas are finished and ready for continuous discovery.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Proto Personas are valuable because they help everyone doing the duty of Product Ownership and UX Research understand different types of customers, so they can make better choices and keep learning what people need, which helps reach the goal of Continuous Discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the Proto Personas, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible, making sure everything gets done and decisions are made. Product Ownership and UX (User Experience) Research duties are consulted, sharing their knowledge and ideas to help shape the Proto Personas. All other duties, like Business Analysis, Development, and Graphic Design, are kept informed so everyone knows what is happening. The activities include customer segmentation, and the skills needed are things like attention to detail, teamwork, and problem-solving. The milestone is continuous discovery, and everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to work well together and finish the Proto Personas.

1. Center for Creative Leadership – "How to Create Psychological Safety on Your Team"

Steps to Produce Deliverable:

1. Product Operations starts the work and makes sure everything gets done by asking everyone to help and keeping them updated.  
2. Scrum Master, Agile Coaching, and Product Operations check the plan and make sure it is right, while other duties like Product Management, UX Design, Development, and QA Analysis share their ideas and help solve problems, especially when making rules for how everyone works together.  
3. Everyone uses skills like talking things out, paying close attention, solving problems, and working together, and they practice making choices, taking responsibility, helping others, making sure everyone feels safe, and letting everyone share ideas so the Psychological Safety Plan helps everyone feel included and safe.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Psychological Safety Plan is valuable because it helps everyone feel safe to share ideas, ask questions, and speak up if something is wrong, which makes it easier to solve problems together and reach the Intake milestone. When people agree on how to work together, it builds trust, helps everyone understand what is expected, and makes it easier to make good decisions, stay organized, and get things done.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Psychological Safety Plan, the Product Operations duty leads the work and is responsible for making sure everything gets done, while the Scrum Master, Agile Coaching, and Product Operations are accountable for the final result. Many other duties, like Product Management, UX Design, Development, and QA Analysis, are consulted to share their ideas and help solve problems, especially when defining team working agreements. All duties are kept informed about progress and decisions. Important skills like negotiation, attention to detail, conflict resolution, and teamwork are used, and everyone practices decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the intake milestone and make sure the Psychological Safety Plan helps everyone feel safe and included.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A QA Analysis is when you carefully check if a product works the way it should by following a plan, looking for mistakes, and making sure everything is clear and organized, using good thinking and communication skills, so the product is ready for launch and meets everyone’s needs.
Online Resources:
1. Ministry of Testing – https://www.ministryoftesting.com/  
2. Guru99 QA Testing Tutorials – https://www.guru99.com/software-testing.html  
3. Test Automation University (by Applitools) – https://testautomationu.applitools.com/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by understanding what the product should do by talking with Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and others who give advice and help solve problems. 
2. Test the product carefully by checking every part, looking for mistakes, and making sure it works the way it should, using skills like attention to detail and problem-solving. 
3. Share what you find with everyone involved, help fix any problems, and make sure the product meets the right standards before it is launched, using good communication and making smart decisions.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A QA Analysis is valuable because it helps everyone understand if the product works the way it should, catches mistakes early, and makes sure everything is ready for launch and acceptance testing, so the duties of business analysis, development, and product ownership can be done with confidence and clear communication.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The QA Analysis duty works with other duties like Business Analysis, Development, and Product Ownership to make sure everything is tested and works well before launch. QA Analysis is responsible for testing, finding problems, and making sure the product meets the right standards, while Product Ownership and QA Analysis are accountable for the final results. Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, UX Research, and UX Writing are consulted to give advice and help solve problems. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others, are kept informed about progress and results. QA Analyses use skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follow practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach important milestones like Quality Assurance, Launch, and Acceptance Testing.

Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Work with other duties like Product Ownership, UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development to gather information and understand what needs to be tested. 
2. Use skills like paying close attention, solving problems, and working well with others to write clear QA Test Cases and create autotests that check if everything works as it should. 
3. Make sure the QA Test Cases are correct and ready by testing them, asking for advice from other duties, and keeping everyone updated about progress, so the product is high quality and ready for launch.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: QA Test Cases are valuable because they help everyone doing their duty make sure the product works the way it should before it is launched, catch mistakes early, and make it easier to talk about what needs to be fixed or improved, so the product is safe and ready for people to use.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The QA Analysis duty works with other duties like Product Ownership, UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development to create the QA Test Cases by using skills such as attention to detail, problem-solving, and teamwork. QA Analysis is responsible and accountable for making sure the QA Test Cases are correct and ready, while Product Ownership also shares accountability. Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, UX Research, and UX Writing are consulted to give advice and information that helps make the QA Test Cases better. All duties, including Development, Graphic Design, and others, are kept informed about the progress and results. The main activities are creating autotests and doing QA testing, and the important milestones are Quality Assurance and Launch. Good practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety help everyone work together well.

2. QA Analysis uses hard skills like attention to detail and problem-solving to write the QA Test Plan, making sure it covers all the important parts and follows good practices like clear decision-making and ownership.  
3. QA Analysis shares the QA Test Plan with all duties, including Business Analysis, Development, and Graphic Design, so everyone knows what is happening, and then uses the plan to do QA testing and help reach milestones like Quality Assurance and Launch.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A QA Test Plan is valuable because it helps everyone understand what needs to be tested, how it will be tested, and when it will be tested, making sure nothing important is missed and helping the group reach the Quality Assurance and Launch milestones with confidence and clear steps.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The QA Analysis duty works with Product Ownership and other duties to create a QA Test Plan by using skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, and teamwork. QA Analysis is responsible for making the QA Test Plan, while Product Ownership and QA Analysis are both accountable for making sure it is done right. Business Analysis, Product Management, UX Design, UX Research, and UX Writing are consulted to give advice and share information that helps make the QA Test Plan better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, and others, are kept informed about the QA Test Plan so everyone knows what is happening. The work involves activities like QA testing and follows practices such as decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach important milestones like Quality Assurance and Launch.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A RACI Chart is a chart that helps everyone know who is supposed to do what, who helps, who gives advice, and who needs to be told about what is happening, so that all duties are clear and people can work together better and make good decisions.
Online Resources:
1. Project Management Institute (PMI) – "How to Create a RACI Chart"  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/how-to-create-raci-chart-8423

2. Atlassian – "RACI Matrix: Responsibility Assignment Matrix Guide"  
https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook/plays/raci-matrix

3. Smartsheet – "How to Create and Use a RACI Matrix"  
https://www.smartsheet.com/raci-diagram-to-define-project-roles
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. First, the Product Operations duty owner talks with everyone to figure out what each duty needs to do, making sure all duties understand their part, especially when starting new ways of working.  
2. Next, Product Operations makes the RACI Chart by writing down who is responsible for each task, who checks the work, who gives advice, and who needs to know what is happening, using clear communication and good organization.  
3. Finally, Product Operations checks with duties like Scrum Master and Agile Coaching to make sure the chart is correct, asks for advice from duties like Product Management, UX Design, and Development, and keeps all duties updated, using problem-solving, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so the chart is ready for the Intake milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A RACI Chart is valuable because it helps everyone know exactly who is responsible for each duty, who needs to be asked for advice, who should be kept informed, and who makes the final decisions, so all the duties get done without confusion and the group can reach the Intake milestone smoothly.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For the RACI Charts, the Product Operations duty owner works with all other duties by making sure everyone knows what they are supposed to do, especially during activities like defining who does what and leading changes using agile ways of working. Product Operations is responsible for getting the RACI Charts done, while the Scrum Master, Agile Coaching, and Product Operations are accountable for making sure everything is correct and finished. Many other duties, like Product Management, UX Design, Development, and more, are consulted to give their input and advice, and all duties are kept informed about progress and decisions. To complete the RACI Charts, everyone uses skills like communication, problem-solving, and organization, and follows practices like making good decisions, taking ownership, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, all while reaching the Intake milestone.

Deliverable Description: A Rainbow Table is a set of instructions that helps you organize and understand research findings by using skills like analyzing data, thinking carefully, and working with others, so you can make smart decisions and keep improving how you design and manage products.
Online Resources:
1. OWASP: https://owasp.org/www-community/Attacks/Using_rainbow_tables

3. GeeksforGeeks: https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/rainbow-table-attack-in-cryptography/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Gather all the research findings and look for patterns or important points by thinking carefully and using data analysis skills.  
2. Ask for advice and ideas from other duties like Product Management, Business Analysis, UX Design, and UX Strategy to make sure the information is correct and helpful.  
3. Organize the findings into a Rainbow Table, making sure everyone is kept up to date, and use good practices like sharing ideas, making decisions together, and making sure everyone feels safe to speak up, so the Rainbow Table is clear, useful, and helps with continuous discovery.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Rainbow Table is valuable because it helps people doing business analysis, product management, product ownership, UX design, and UX strategy see patterns in research findings, so everyone can make better decisions together, stay organized, and keep learning as they work toward continuous discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, UX (User Experience) Research, is accountable and responsible for making sure a Rainbow Table is finished by analyzing research findings and using skills like critical thinking, data analysis, and teamwork. Other duties, such as Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, while all duties, including Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, and more, are kept informed about progress. Everyone works together by sharing ideas, making decisions, and using practices like ownership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the milestone of continuous discovery and make sure the Rainbow Tables are useful and complete.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A refined backlog is a clear and organized list of tasks and ideas that need to be done, where each item is explained in detail, sorted by what is most important, and updated often so everyone knows what to work on next and how much effort it will take.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org – "Refining the Product Backlog": https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/refining-product-backlog  
2. Atlassian – "Backlog grooming or refinement": https://www.atlassian.com/agile/scrum/backlog-refinement  
3. Mountain Goat Software – "Product Backlog Refinement": https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/agile/scrum/backlog-refinement
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by looking at all the tasks and deciding which ones are most important, using problem-solving and good communication to talk about what needs to be done first.  
2. Work together to break down each task, figure out how much work it will take, and make sure every task is clear and easy to understand, using organization and decision-making skills.  
3. Keep checking and updating the list of tasks as new information comes in, making sure everyone knows about changes, and always looking for ways to make the list better by listening to advice and helping each other.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Refined Backlog helps cross-functional groups because it keeps everyone focused on the most important work, makes sure all duties are clear, and helps people see what needs to be done next so they can reach the Continuous Discovery milestone faster and with fewer mistakes.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Refined Backlog, the Product Ownership and Scrum Master duties work together by making sure all the tasks are clear, organized, and ready to be worked on, while Business Analysis helps break down what needs to be done and why. These duties are accountable for making sure the Refined Backlog is always up to date and useful, and they are responsible for doing activities like setting priorities, estimating how much work things will take, and keeping track of progress. All other duties, like UX Design, Development, and QA Analysis, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, and everyone is kept informed about changes and updates. The main skills used are problem-solving, communication, organization, and decision-making, and the practices include making good choices, taking ownership, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. The big milestone is Continuous Discovery, which means always learning and improving the Refined Backlog as new information comes in.
