Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Release Plan is a set of clear steps and information that explains how to bring a new product or feature to customers, including what the product is, who it is for, how it will be marketed, when it will be ready, and what needs to be done to make it successful, using skills like research, planning, communication, and problem-solving.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian Agile Coach – Release Planning  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/release-planning

2. Scrum.org – Release Planning in Scrum  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/release-planning-scrum

3. Project Management Institute (PMI) – Release Planning: Best Practices  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/release-planning-best-practices-11113
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by doing market research and customer segmentation to learn about the people who might use the product and what they need. 
2. Next, define the release vision and scope by deciding what the product will do and what features it will have, using advice from different duties like UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development. 
3. Then, develop the go-to-market strategy and product positioning, making sure everyone is kept informed and involved by sharing updates and listening to feedback, while using good decision-making, organization, and working together with respect and clear communication.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Release Plan is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand what needs to be done, when it should happen, and why it matters, so people can work together, make smart choices, and reach important goals like discovering new ideas, creating a clear vision, and launching something new.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Release Plan, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible for making sure all the steps are done right, like developing the go-to-market strategy, defining the vision, and setting the scope, while using skills such as decision-making, organization, and teamwork. Other duties, like Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, and Development, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge during important activities and milestones like Continuous Discovery, Vision, and Launch. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress and updates of the Release Plan, so everyone knows what is happening and can work together smoothly using practices like ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment.

Workshop That Produces the Deliverable: "Product Release Vision, Scope, and Roadmap Workshop"
Deliverable Description: A Release-level Scope is a clear plan that explains what will be included in a product release, what needs to be done, and when it will be finished, using careful thinking, good communication, and smart decision-making to make sure everyone understands and agrees on what is most important.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian Agile Coach – Release Planning  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/release-planning

2. Scrum.org – Release Planning in Scrum  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/release-planning-scrum

3. Project Management Institute (PMI) – Release Management Best Practices  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/release-management-best-practices-11013
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Product Management makes sure the Release-level Scope is clear and matches the goals by talking with everyone and collecting ideas and feedback from duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, Business Analysis, and Development. 
2. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept updated about what is happening and help by sharing information, solving problems, and making decisions together. 
3. Everyone uses skills like good communication, staying organized, and working well with others to finish the Release Scope Definition and reach the milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Release-level Scope is valuable because it helps everyone understand what needs to be done and when, so all duties can work together smoothly to reach the goal. It makes sure nothing important is missed, helps people make good choices, and keeps everyone focused on the same plan. This helps achieve the milestone by making sure all the work fits together and moves forward in the right way.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For a Release-level Scope, the Product Management duty is accountable and responsible for making sure the scope is clear and meets the goals, while other duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, Business Analysis, and Development are consulted to give their ideas and feedback. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress. Everyone works together by sharing information, making decisions, solving problems, and using skills like communication, organization, and teamwork to reach the scope milestone.

Deliverable Description: A Research Analysis is a report that explains what was learned from studying people’s needs and behaviors, using activities like surveys, interviews, and testing ideas, and it helps people make smart choices by showing patterns, facts, and suggestions in a clear and organized way.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – Guide to Data and Research Analysis  
https://hbr.org/2017/09/a-refresher-on-regression-analysis

https://www.coursera.org/learn/research-methods

Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information by doing activities like surveys, interviews, observing people, and testing how they use things. 
2. Look at the information by sorting cards, finding patterns, and using math to understand what the data means. 
3. Share what you learned by working with other duties, asking for advice, and making sure everyone knows the results, especially during important times like when new ideas are being discovered or tested to see if they work.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Research Analysis is valuable because it helps people doing different duties understand what users need, spot patterns in data, and make smart choices about what to build or fix next, which helps everyone reach important goals like making sure new ideas work well and are easy to use.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: A UX (User Experience) Research duty owner works with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, and UX (User Experience) Strategy to complete a Research Analysis by doing activities such as surveys, usability testing, and analyzing research findings, while using skills like critical thinking, teamwork, and data analysis; the UX (User Experience) Research duty is accountable and responsible for making sure the Research Analysis is done well, while Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing are consulted to give advice and feedback, and all other duties are kept informed about the progress and results, especially during important milestones like Continuous Discovery and Acceptance Testing, using practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Research Plan is a set of clear steps that explains how to find answers to important questions by choosing the right research methods, like surveys or interviews, and planning activities such as card sorting, usability testing, and observing people, while using skills like problem-solving, organization, and communication to make good decisions and learn new things.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "How to Write a Research Plan"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/research-plan/

2. Interaction Design Foundation – "How to Create a UX Research Plan"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/how-to-create-a-ux-research-plan

3. UX Collective – "How to Write a UX Research Plan"  
https://uxdesign.cc/how-to-write-a-ux-research-plan-8e2935e4c1e6
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by gathering information through activities like card sorting, secondary research, and primary research to understand what users need and want. 
2. Choose the best research methods, design experiments, and plan how to test ideas using surveys, observing people (ethnography), usability testing, user acceptance testing, and prototype concept testing. 
3. Work closely with other duties such as Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy to create and review the Research Plan, making sure everyone shares advice, stays informed, and uses skills like problem-solving, communication, and attention to detail while following good practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to keep discovering new things.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Research Plan is valuable because it helps everyone doing business analysis, product management, product ownership, UX design, and UX strategy stay organized, make smart decisions, and work together to find out what users need, so they can keep improving the product and reach the goal of continuous discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: A UX (User Experience) Research duty owner works with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, and UX (User Experience) Strategy to create a Research Plan by doing activities such as card sorting, choosing research methods, designing experiments, and testing ideas with users. The UX (User Experience) Research and Product Ownership duties are accountable for making sure the Research Plan is done right, while UX (User Experience) Research is responsible for doing the work. Duties like Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress. Everyone uses skills like attention to detail, teamwork, problem-solving, and communication, and follows practices such as decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the milestone of continuous discovery.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Research Report is a written document that explains what was learned from studying people, their needs, and how they use things, using activities like interviews, surveys, and testing ideas, so others can understand the findings and make better decisions.
Online Resources:
1. Purdue Online Writing Lab (OWL) – Research Reports  
https://owl.purdue.edu/owl/subject_specific_writing/writing_in_the_social_sciences/research_report_sections/index.html

2. University of Southern California Libraries – Organizing Your Social Sciences Research Paper  
https://libguides.usc.edu/writingguide/researchreport

3. Scribbr – How to Write a Research Paper  
https://www.scribbr.com/research-paper/research-paper-structure/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by planning and choosing which research activities to do, like interviews, surveys, and observing people using a product. 
2. Carry out the research by talking to users, watching how they use things, testing ideas, and collecting information from both new studies and existing sources. 
3. Work together with other duties to look at the findings, write the Research Report, and share the results, making sure to listen to advice, use good communication, and keep everyone updated so the research helps improve the product.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Research Report is valuable because it helps people doing business analysis, product management, product ownership, UX design, and UX strategy understand what users need and how they behave, so they can make better decisions, solve problems faster, and keep improving the product as they work toward continuous discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: A UX (User Experience) Research duty owner works with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, and UX Strategy to create a Research Report by doing activities such as interviews, surveys, and usability testing. The UX Research and Product Ownership duties are accountable for making sure the Research Report is done well, while UX Research is responsible for carrying out the research and writing the report. Duties like Product Management, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing are consulted to give advice and feedback during the process. All other duties, including Development, QA Analysis, and Project Management, are kept informed about the progress and results. Everyone uses skills like critical thinking, teamwork, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the milestone of Continuous Discovery.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Risk Analysis is when you carefully look at a project to find out what could go wrong, think about how likely those problems are, and decide what to do to stop them or fix them if they happen, using skills like paying attention to details, thinking things through, working with others, and making good decisions.
Online Resources:
1. Project Management Institute (PMI) – Risk Analysis Resources  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/risk-analysis-techniques-project-management-8323

2. Coursera – Risk Management Courses (including risk analysis tutorials)  
https://www.coursera.org/courses?query=risk%20analysis

3. Harvard Business Review – Risk Analysis Articles and Guides  
https://hbr.org/topic/risk-analysis
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information about the project and talk with everyone who has knowledge or advice about possible problems. 
2. Carefully look for things that could go wrong, think about how likely they are, and decide how big the problems could be. 
3. Write down the risks, share them with everyone who needs to know, and make sure the person in charge checks and approves the Risk Analysis before moving forward.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Risk Analysis is valuable because it helps people spot problems before they happen, so everyone can make smart choices, stay organized, and work together to keep the project on track and reach important goals.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For a Risk Analysis, the Project Management duty makes sure everything gets done and is responsible for checking for problems that could happen. Product Management is accountable, meaning they make the final decisions and make sure the Risk Analysis is right. Product Ownership, Scrum Master, Business Analysis, UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Agile Coaching, Account Management, and Product Management are consulted, so they give advice and share their knowledge to help make the Risk Analysis better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, and all the UX and Account Management duties, are informed, so they know what is happening and can stay updated. Everyone uses skills like paying attention to details, thinking carefully, solving problems, and working together, and they follow good practices like making decisions, taking ownership, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. Risk Analyses are done at every product milestone to keep things on track.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Workshop That Produces the Deliverable: "Product Release Vision, Scope, and Roadmap Workshop"
Deliverable Description: A Roadmap is a plan that shows what needs to be done, when it should happen, and how to keep track of big goals and important steps, using careful thinking, good organization, and clear communication to make sure everything moves forward the right way.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian Roadmap Guide – https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/roadmaps  
2. ProductPlan Roadmap Resources – https://www.productplan.com/learn/roadmap-examples/  
3. Aha! Roadmap Tutorials – https://www.aha.io/roadmapping/guide
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Product Management, Product Ownership, and Project Management talk with other duties to decide what needs to be done and set big goals, like the Vision and important milestones.  
2. They keep the Roadmap up to date by checking progress on big tasks (Epics) and milestones, making sure everyone knows what is happening and what comes next.  
3. All duties, like UX Design, Development, and Business Analysis, share their advice and knowledge, while everyone uses skills like paying attention, solving problems, and talking clearly, and follows good practices like making decisions, taking responsibility, and helping each other so the Roadmap is clear and everyone can do their part.

Deliverable Description: A Sitemap is a map that shows how all the pages and information on a website are organized, helping people plan, test, and improve how easy it is to find things, while using skills like problem-solving, creativity, and clear communication.
Online Resources:
1. Google Search Central – Sitemaps documentation  
https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/sitemaps/overview

2. Yoast – What is an XML sitemap and how do I create one?

3. Moz – Sitemaps: A Beginner’s Guide  
https://moz.com/learn/seo/sitemaps
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Work together to plan how all the pages and information on the website will be organized, using skills like problem-solving, attention to detail, and good communication. 
2. Build the Sitemap by drawing out the structure and making sure it matches what people need, with duties like UX Design and UX Research doing the main work and getting advice from others like Product Management and Graphic Design. 
3. Test the Sitemap to see if it makes sense and is easy to use, making changes if needed, and always keeping everyone updated and making sure everyone feels comfortable sharing their ideas.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Sitemap is valuable because it shows how all the pages and information connect, making it easier for everyone doing business analysis, product ownership, and UX research to organize ideas, spot problems, and plan the best experience for users, which helps everyone reach the experience design and requirements milestone faster and with less confusion.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner for the Sitemap is UX (User Experience) Design and UX (User Experience) Research, who work closely with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Strategy, and UX (User Experience) Writing to organize and test how information is set up on a website. Business Analysis and UX (User Experience) Research are accountable for making sure the Sitemap is correct, while UX (User Experience) Strategy and UX (User Experience) Research are responsible for doing the work, like developing the information structure and testing the Sitemap. Many other duties, such as Product Management, Development, Graphic Design, and Information Security Analysis, are consulted to give advice and feedback, and everyone involved is kept informed about progress. To make a good Sitemap, everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, attention to detail, and communication, and they follow practices like making decisions together, taking ownership, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. The main milestones are Experience Design and Requirements, which help guide the work and make sure the Sitemap meets everyone’s needs.

Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by quickly coming up with lots of ideas for the Sketches, using your imagination and drawing simple pictures to show how things might look or work.  
2. Share your Sketches with others who have different duties, like giving advice, sharing what they know, and helping you make the Sketches better, while listening to everyone’s thoughts and making sure everyone feels comfortable to speak up.  
3. Use problem-solving, good communication, and decision-making to improve the Sketches, making sure they are clear, helpful, and creative, and keep everyone updated on your progress until the Sketches are ready for the Experience Design milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Sketches are valuable because they help people with different duties quickly share and test ideas, making it easier to spot problems and find the best solutions together, which helps everyone reach the experience design milestone faster and with better results.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner for Sketches is UX (User Experience) Design, who is accountable and responsible for making sure the Sketches are done well and on time. Other duties like UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted, which means they give advice and share their knowledge to make the Sketches better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress. Everyone works together by sharing ideas quickly, using skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and following practices like making good decisions, taking ownership, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share their thoughts. The main milestone is Experience Design, and everyone’s input helps make the Sketches clear, useful, and creative.

Deliverable Description: A Sprint Demo Presentation is when you show what you have made or learned during a project sprint by creating a clear and organized presentation, explaining your work, answering questions, and helping others understand your progress, using skills like storytelling, problem-solving, communication, and time management.

3. Then, the accountable duties check that the presentation is complete and correct, the consulted duties give helpful advice and feedback, and the informed duties are kept updated about the progress and results of the Sprint Demo Presentation.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Sprint Demo Presentation is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty see what has been built, share feedback, and make decisions together, so the group can reach the next product milestone faster and with better results.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For a Sprint Demo Presentation, the duty owner and other duties work together by creating presentations and leading the demo, using skills like storytelling, public speaking, and problem-solving while following practices like decision-making and psychological safety. The accountable duties, which include all duties and product ownership, make sure the Sprint Demo Presentation meets all product milestones. The responsible duties, including all duties and the Scrum Master, actually do the work to prepare and present. Consulted duties, such as account management, project management, and product management, give advice and feedback to help improve the presentation. Informed duties, like business analysis, development, graphic design, and many others, are kept updated about the progress and results of the Sprint Demo Presentation.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: Sprint Goals are clear instructions that help everyone know what needs to be finished during a sprint, including planning how much work can be done, figuring out how fast things are getting done, and making sure everyone is working together, making good decisions, and staying organized so the most important tasks get done on time.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org – "Sprint Goal"  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/what-is-a-sprint-goal

2. Scrum Alliance – "How to Create a Sprint Goal"  
https://resources.scrumalliance.org/Article/how-create-sprint-goal

3. Mountain Goat Software – "Sprint Goals: Why, How, and Examples"  
https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/sprint-goals-why-how-and-examples
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Estimate how much work each activity will take by talking with everyone and using past work to help guess.  
2. Plan what to do in the sprint by choosing the most important tasks, making sure everyone understands, and writing clear goals.  
3. Check how fast the group works by looking at past sprints, keep track of progress at each milestone, and help everyone solve problems, share ideas, and make good decisions together.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Sprint Goals are valuable because they help everyone understand what needs to be finished and keep all duties focused on the same important tasks, making it easier to reach the milestone together and see progress along the way.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The Scrum Master duty works with all other duties to complete the Sprint Goals by making sure everyone knows what to do, helping plan the work, and checking progress at each product milestone. The Scrum Master and Agile Coaching duties are accountable for the Sprint Goals, while the Scrum Master and Product Ownership duties are responsible for doing the work. Many other duties, like Product Management, UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development, are consulted to give advice and share ideas, and all duties are kept informed about what is happening. To finish the Sprint Goals, everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety.
Required Activities: Level of Effort Estimation,Sprint Planning,Velocity Calculation

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Sprint Plan is a set of instructions that helps you organize what needs to be done, keep track of progress, and figure out how much work can be finished in a short period of time, using skills like planning, problem-solving, and communication, while making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas and take responsibility for their part.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org - Sprint Planning: https://www.scrum.org/resources/sprint-planning
2. Atlassian Agile Coach - Sprint Planning: https://www.atlassian.com/agile/scrum/sprint-planning
3. Mountain Goat Software - Sprint Planning Meeting: https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/agile/scrum/sprint-planning-meeting
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The Scrum Master leads everyone in a meeting called Sprint Planning, where they talk about what needs to be done and decide what work to finish during the sprint. 
2. The Scrum Master keeps track of how the work is going by checking in often, helping solve problems, and making sure everyone knows what is happening and what needs to be done next. 
3. The Scrum Master figures out how fast the group is working by calculating velocity, and uses this information to help plan future sprints and make sure everyone is moving toward important milestones like Intake and other product goals. Duties use skills like talking clearly, making good choices, helping each other, and making sure everyone feels safe and included.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Sprint Plan is valuable because it helps everyone understand what needs to be done, keeps all duties organized, and makes sure the group is working toward the same goal for each product milestone. By using a Sprint Plan, people can track progress, solve problems together, and make better decisions, which helps everyone finish their duties on time and reach important milestones.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, Scrum Master, works with all duties to create the Sprint Plan by leading activities like Sprint Planning, tracking progress, and calculating velocity, using skills such as meeting facilitation, problem-solving, and communication. The Scrum Master and Agile Coaching duties are accountable for making sure the Sprint Plan is done right, while the Scrum Master and Product Ownership duties are responsible for doing the work. Account Management, Product Management, Business Analysis, and Project Management duties are consulted to give advice and share information, and all other duties are kept informed about the Sprint Plan’s progress and milestones like Intake and all product milestones. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to work together and reach the goals.

Workshop That Produces the Deliverable: Sprint Retrospective Workshop,Project Phase Retrospective Workshop
Deliverable Description: Sprint Retro Action Items are a list of clear steps that help everyone improve how they work together by talking about what went well, what could be better, and what to try next time, using good listening, sharing ideas, making decisions, and making sure everyone feels safe and included.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org - Sprint Retrospective: https://www.scrum.org/resources/what-is-a-sprint-retrospective  
2. Atlassian Agile Coach - Sprint Retrospective Guide: https://www.atlassian.com/agile/scrum/retrospectives  
3. Mountain Goat Software - Sprint Retrospective Tips: https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/sprint-retrospectives-ideas-for-action
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The Scrum Master leads the Sprint Retrospective meeting by asking everyone to share what went well, what could be better, and ideas for improvement, using skills like speaking clearly, listening, and solving problems. 
2. The Scrum Master helps everyone decide on a few action items to make things better, making sure everyone feels safe to share and that decisions are fair and clear. 
3. The Scrum Master keeps track of the action items, checks in with everyone who gave advice or feedback, and makes sure everyone knows how things are going and what changes are being made for the next product milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Sprint Retro Action Items are valuable because they help everyone remember what needs to be improved and what went well, so people can work better together and reach the next goal faster. By writing down these action items, each duty can see what to focus on, solve problems, and make sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, which helps everyone grow and succeed.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The Scrum Master is the duty owner and is accountable and responsible for making sure Sprint Retro Action Items are completed by leading the Sprint Retrospective, using skills like meeting facilitation, public speaking, and problem-solving, while practicing decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety; other duties such as Product Management, UX Design, Development, and QA Analysis are consulted to give advice and share feedback, and all duties are kept informed about the progress and results, so everyone works together, shares ideas, and helps improve how things are done for all product milestones.

Deliverable Description: Stakeholder Feedback is when you collect and share opinions from people who use or care about a product, making sure their ideas help improve how the product works and feels by testing it, thinking carefully, solving problems, and communicating clearly during important steps like acceptance testing and launch.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – "How to Give Feedback to People Who Cry, Yell, or Get Defensive"  
https://hbr.org/2014/10/how-to-give-feedback-to-people-who-cry-yell-or-get-defensive

2. MindTools – "Giving Feedback"  
https://www.mindtools.com/azb6g5a/giving-feedback

3. Center for Creative Leadership – "How to Give Feedback That Works"  
https://www.ccl.org/articles/leading-effectively-articles/feedback-that-works/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Gather feedback from people who use the product by asking questions and watching how they use it during User Acceptance Testing.  
2. Share what you learn with everyone doing the different duties, like Product Management, Product Ownership, UX Design, UX Strategy, and Business Analysis, so they can give advice and help understand the feedback.  
3. Use the feedback to make smart choices about what to change or improve, making sure everyone knows what is happening and working together by using good communication, problem-solving, and paying attention to details, while following important steps like Acceptance Testing, Continuous Discovery, Launch, and Experience Design.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Stakeholder Feedback is valuable because it helps everyone doing their duty understand what users really need and if the product works well for them, so people can make better decisions, fix problems early, and make sure the product is ready for important steps like Acceptance Testing, Continuous Discovery, Launch, and Experience Design.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete Stakeholder Feedback, the UX (User Experience) Research duty is responsible for gathering and understanding what people think about the product, while Product Ownership is accountable for making sure the feedback is used the right way. Product Management, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed so everyone knows what is happening. The duties work together by using skills like attention to detail, problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, following practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety, and reaching milestones such as Acceptance Testing, Continuous Discovery, Launch, and Experience Design.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Statement of Work gives clear instructions for starting a project, including what needs to be done, how to work with others, how to make good decisions, and how to keep everyone safe and included, so everyone knows what is expected and can do their duty well.
Online Resources:
1. Project Management Institute (PMI) – "How to Write a Statement of Work"  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/how-to-write-statement-of-work-11013

2. Smartsheet – "How to Write a Statement of Work for Any Industry"  
https://www.smartsheet.com/how-write-statement-work-sow

3. Atlassian – "How to Write a Statement of Work (SOW): Templates & Examples"  
https://www.atlassian.com/work-management/project-management/project-planning/statement-of-work
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by gathering all the important information needed for the Statement of Work during the kickoff or intake milestone, making sure everyone’s ideas and needs are heard and written down.  
2. Share this information with all the different duties, like Business Analysis, Project Management, UX Design, Development, and others, so they can give their expert advice and help solve any problems.  
3. Use skills like leading meetings, talking things out, paying close attention, and working well with others, while following good practices like making decisions together, taking responsibility, helping each other, feeling safe to share ideas, and always trying to get better, so the Statement of Work is clear, correct, and ready for everyone to use.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Statement of Work is valuable because it clearly explains what needs to be done, when it should be finished, and who is responsible, which helps everyone understand their duties and work together smoothly to reach the Intake milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete a Statement of Work, the Business Analysis duty is responsible for making sure all the right information is gathered and shared, while Project Management is accountable for making sure everything is done correctly and on time. Other duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give their expert advice and help solve problems. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about what is happening. Everyone works together by using skills like meeting facilitation, negotiation, attention to detail, and teamwork, and by following practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, empowerment, and continuous growth, especially during the kickoff or intake milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A storyboard is a set of pictures and notes that show how a story or idea will happen step by step, helping people plan, organize, and explain their thoughts clearly so everyone understands what will happen next.
Online Resources:
1. Adobe Creative Cloud Tutorials – Storyboarding: https://helpx.adobe.com/creative-cloud/how-to/storyboard.html  
2. StudioBinder – Storyboard Examples & Templates: https://www.studiobinder.com/blog/storyboard-examples/  
3. Boords – How to Create a Storyboard: https://boords.com/learn/how-to-create-a-storyboard
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information from everyone’s duties, like research, writing, and business analysis, to understand what the Storyboard needs. 
2. Use storytelling, drawing, and attention to detail to create the Storyboard, while asking for advice from other duties like product management, development, and graphic design to make it better. 
3. Share updates and important choices with all duties, making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, takes responsibility, and helps each other, so the Storyboard meets all the requirements and is finished on time.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A storyboard is valuable because it helps everyone see the plan for a project in simple pictures and words, making it easier to understand what needs to happen next. By using a storyboard, people can share ideas, make better decisions together, and feel comfortable speaking up, which helps everyone take responsibility and show leadership. Storyboards also help keep things organized and make sure everyone is working toward the same goal, which is important for reaching the milestone in UX design.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: A UX (User Experience) Design duty owner works with many other duties to make a Storyboard by using skills like storytelling, attention to detail, and teamwork. The UX (User Experience) Strategy duty is both accountable and responsible, making sure the Storyboard meets all requirements and is finished on time. Duties like UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted, which means they give advice and share their knowledge to help make the Storyboard better. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress and important decisions. Everyone uses good practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to work together and reach the requirements milestone for the Storyboard.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A System Architecture Diagram shows how all the parts of a system work together, using clear pictures and labels to explain how information moves and how different pieces connect, so everyone can understand how to build, use, and improve the system.
Online Resources:
1. AWS Architecture Center – https://aws.amazon.com/architecture/
2. Microsoft Azure Architecture Center – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/
3. Lucidchart Tutorials – https://www.lucidchart.com/pages/tutorial/architecture-diagram
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Solutions Architecture starts by talking with Product Ownership and other duties like UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development to understand what the system needs to do and what problems it should solve. 
2. Solutions Architecture uses skills like problem-solving and logical thinking to draw the System Architecture Diagram, showing how all the parts of the system connect and work together, and checks with Product Ownership to make sure everything is correct and meets the needs. 
3. Solutions Architecture shares the diagram with all duties, listens to their advice and feedback, makes changes if needed, and keeps everyone updated until the Architecture milestone is reached and the diagram is finished.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A System Architecture Diagram is valuable because it shows how all the parts of a project fit together, making it easier for people with different duties like business analysis, development, and product ownership to understand what needs to be built and how everything connects. This helps everyone make better decisions, stay organized, and work together to reach the architecture milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty of Solutions Architecture is responsible for creating the System Architecture Diagram, working closely with Product Ownership, who is accountable along with Solutions Architecture to make sure the diagrams are correct and meet the needs. Solutions Architecture uses skills like problem-solving, logical thinking, and teamwork, and follows practices like decision-making and ownership. While making the System Architecture Diagram, Solutions Architecture consults with duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Business Analysis, Product Management, and Scrum Master to get advice and feedback. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress and results. The main milestone is Architecture, and everyone works together by sharing information, asking questions, and making decisions to finish the System Architecture Diagram.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Task Analysis is a clear plan that explains each step needed to study how people use a product, including making research plans, testing ideas, and asking questions, so you can find ways to make the product better and easier to use.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – Task Analysis: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/task-analysis/
2. Interaction Design Foundation – Task Analysis: https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/topics/task-analysis
3. Usability.gov – Task Analysis: https://www.usability.gov/how-to-and-tools/methods/task-analysis.html
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. The UX Research duty starts by making a research plan, choosing the best ways to test ideas, like A/B testing, concept testing, and usability testing.  
2. The Product Ownership duty checks that everything is done correctly and follows the plan, while duties like Product Management, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing give advice and share their thoughts.  
3. All duties keep each other updated, make decisions together, and use good habits like being responsible, helping others, making everyone feel safe to share, and letting people try new things, so they can keep learning and improving.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Task Analysis is valuable because it helps everyone understand exactly what needs to be done, breaks big problems into smaller steps, and makes it easier to find better ways to work together, which helps reach the Continuous Discovery milestone faster and with fewer mistakes.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For the Task Analysis, the UX (User Experience) Research duty is responsible for creating research plans and using skills like A/B prototype testing, concept testing, and usability testing, while the Product Ownership duty is accountable for making sure everything is done right. Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing are consulted to give advice and share ideas, and all other duties are kept informed about what is happening. Everyone works together by sharing information, making decisions, and using practices like ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the milestone of continuous discovery.

Good practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment help everyone work together and reach the requirements milestone for Task Flows.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Task-Level Scope is a set of clear instructions that explains what needs to be done, how to do it, and what the finished work should look like, including writing down the requirements and creating acceptance criteria so everyone understands what is expected and how to know when the work is done correctly.
Online Resources:
1. Project Management Institute (PMI) – "Defining Project Scope"  
https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/defining-project-scope-8267

2. Wrike Blog – "How to Define Project Scope"  
https://www.wrike.com/blog/how-to-define-project-scope/

3. Smartsheet – "How to Write a Project Scope Statement"  
https://www.smartsheet.com/how-write-project-scope-statement
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. The Business Analysis duty works with other duties like Development, UX Writing, UX Strategy, UX Research, UX Design, Solutions Architecture, Scrum Master, QA Analysis, Project Management, Product Ownership, Product Operations, Product Management, Information Security Analysis, and Graphic Design to gather ideas and advice for writing clear requirements and creating acceptance criteria.  
2. Business Analysis and Product Ownership check that the requirements are correct, while Business Analysis writes the requirements and acceptance criteria, using skills like problem-solving, attention to detail, communication, and teamwork.  
3. All duties are kept updated about progress, and everyone follows good practices like making decisions together, taking responsibility, helping others, making sure everyone feels safe to share, and letting people do their best work, so the requirements milestone is reached.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Task-Level Scope helps everyone doing their duty know exactly what needs to be done and how to do it, so people can work together better, avoid confusion, and finish the work the right way to reach the milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For Task-Level Scopes, the Business Analysis duty owner works with other duties like Development, UX Writing, UX Strategy, UX Research, UX Design, Solutions Architecture, Scrum Master, QA Analysis, Project Management, Product Ownership, Product Operations, Product Management, Information Security Analysis, and Graphic Design to write requirements and create acceptance criteria. Business Analysis and Product Ownership are accountable for making sure the requirements are correct, while Business Analysis is responsible for doing the work. Duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and share ideas. All duties are kept informed about progress. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, teamwork, attention to detail, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the requirements milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Taxonomy is a way to organize and group information so it is easy to find and understand, and creating one means you need to pay close attention to details, think carefully, work with others, and use creativity and problem-solving to decide how things should be sorted and labeled, making sure everyone can use and understand the information easily.
Online Resources:
1. The Taxonomy Toolkit by the Australian Museum  
https://australian.museum/learn/teachers/taxonomy-toolkit/

2. Encyclopedia of Life – Learning + Education: Taxonomy  
https://education.eol.org/lesson_plans/2-5_Taxonomy.html

3. Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History – Introduction to Taxonomy  
https://naturalhistory.si.edu/education/teaching-resources/life-science/introduction-taxonomy
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by planning how the information will be organized, with the UX Design duty leading and making sure everything fits together. 
2. Check that the plan matches the goals by having Business Analysis review and approve the work, while UX Strategy helps decide the best way to organize the design. 
3. Ask for advice from duties like UX Research, UX Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis, and keep everyone updated on progress. Use problem-solving, creativity, and good communication, and make sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so the information architecture is strong and ready for the Experience Design milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A taxonomy is valuable because it helps everyone organize information in a clear way, making it easier to find and understand what is important for experience design. By using a taxonomy, people doing business analysis, product ownership, and user experience work can make better decisions, solve problems faster, and create designs that are easy for everyone to use. This helps the group reach the experience design milestone because everyone can work together, share ideas, and make sure the information is set up in the best way for users.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For an Experience Design Taxonomy, the UX (User Experience) Design duty is the main duty owner and works closely with other duties to make sure everything fits together well. Business Analysis is accountable, making sure the work meets the goals, while UX (User Experience) Strategy is responsible for planning how the design will work. UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress. Everyone uses skills like problem-solving, creativity, and teamwork, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the Experience Design milestone by developing strong information architecture.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Team Charter gives clear instructions for how a group will work together to lead an Agile Transformation, including how to make decisions, solve problems, communicate, and support each other, so everyone knows what is expected and can do their best work.
Online Resources:
1. MindTools – Team Charters: https://www.mindtools.com/a4wo118/team-charters  
2. Atlassian – How to Create a Team Charter: https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook/plays/team-charter  
3. Harvard Business Review – How to Create a Team Charter: https://hbr.org/2012/04/how-to-create-a-team-charter
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Product Operations gathers information from everyone and organizes the work, making sure all steps are followed.  
2. Agile Coaching and Product Operations check that the Team Charter is done well and meets the goals, while other duties like UX Design, Project Management, Business Analysis, and Development give advice and share their knowledge to make the Charter better.  
3. All duties are kept updated about the progress, and everyone uses good communication, teamwork, problem-solving, and decision-making, while following practices like ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to finish the Team Charter and reach the intake milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Team Charter is valuable because it helps everyone understand the group’s purpose, how to work together, and what is expected, making it easier to solve problems, make decisions, and reach goals during the Intake milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For a Team Charter, the Product Operations duty leads the work by making sure everyone follows the right steps and keeps things organized, while Agile Coaching and Product Operations are accountable for making sure the Team Charter is done well and meets the goals. Product Operations is responsible for doing the main work, like gathering information and putting the Charter together. Many other duties, like UX Design, Project Management, Business Analysis, Development, and more, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, helping to make the Charter stronger. All duties are kept informed about what is happening, so everyone knows the progress and can help if needed. The work involves activities like leading Agile transformation, using skills such as communication, teamwork, problem-solving, and decision-making, and following practices like ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the intake milestone and create a strong Team Charter.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Workshop That Produces the Deliverable: Team Process and Kanban Board Training Workshop
Deliverable Description: A Team Process Map shows the steps and rules for how people work together, make decisions, solve problems, and get things done, using clear instructions and good habits like listening, sharing ideas, and helping each other, so everyone feels safe, included, and ready to grow.
Online Resources:
1. Lucidchart - "How to Create a Process Map"  
https://www.lucidchart.com/blog/how-to-make-a-process-map

2. ASQ (American Society for Quality) - "Process Mapping"  
https://asq.org/quality-resources/process-mapping

3. Smartsheet - "How to Create a Process Map: A Step-by-Step Guide"  
https://www.smartsheet.com/how-create-process-map-step-by-step-guide
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by having the Product Operations duty gather everyone to talk about how work should be done, making sure to listen to advice from other duties like Agile Coaching, Product Ownership, Product Management, UX Design, and Development.  
2. Product Operations leads the group in deciding the best way to organize tasks, solve problems, and make changes, using skills like talking clearly, working together, and helping everyone feel safe to share ideas.  
3. As the process is mapped out, Product Operations keeps all duties updated on progress and important news, making sure everyone understands what is happening and can help reach important steps, like Intake, by using good decision-making and ownership.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Team Process Map is valuable because it shows everyone the steps and rules for how to work together, helps people make good choices, and makes sure everyone knows what to do and when, so the group can start new projects smoothly and reach the Intake milestone faster and with less confusion.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete a Team Process Map, the Product Operations duty is the owner and is accountable for making sure everything gets done right, while also being responsible for carrying out the main activities like defining the process and leading changes. Other duties, such as Agile Coaching, Product Ownership, Product Management, UX Design, Development, and more, are consulted to give advice and share their expertise, helping Product Operations make good decisions. All duties are kept informed about progress and important updates, so everyone knows what is happening and can work together smoothly. The work involves skills like meeting facilitation, problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to make sure everyone feels included and empowered as the Team Process Map reaches each milestone, like Intake.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: The Technical Architecture Specifications are a set of clear instructions that explain how to design and build a system, making sure every part works well together and meets the needs of the project, while using careful thinking, good communication, and strong organization skills.
Online Resources:
1. Microsoft Learn – "Solution Architectures"  
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/guide/architecture-styles/

2. AWS Architecture Center – "AWS Well-Architected Framework"  
https://aws.amazon.com/architecture/well-architected/

3. TOGAF® Standard Documentation (The Open Group)  
https://www.opengroup.org/togaf
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Solutions Architecture works with Business Analysis, Development, and Product Ownership duties to gather information and understand what the product needs to do.  
2. Solutions Architecture uses problem-solving, logical thinking, and clear communication to design how the product will work, making sure to ask for advice from Business Analysis, Product Management, Scrum Master, and UX duties.  
3. Solutions Architecture checks that the Technical Architecture Specifications are correct and complete, keeps everyone updated on progress, and works with Product Ownership to make sure the final result is ready for the Architecture milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Technical Architecture Specifications are valuable because they show everyone how the parts of a project will work together, making it easier for people with different duties like business analysis, development, and product ownership to understand what needs to be built and how to do it. This helps everyone make good decisions, stay organized, and reach the architecture milestone by making sure the project is clear, safe to share ideas, and everyone feels responsible for their part.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, Solutions Architecture, works with other duties like Business Analysis, Development, and Product Ownership to create the Technical Architecture Specifications by using skills such as problem-solving, logical thinking, and teamwork. Solutions Architecture is responsible for making sure the Technical Architecture Specifications are correct and complete, while Product Ownership and Solutions Architecture are accountable for the final result. Business Analysis, Product Management, Scrum Master, and UX duties are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, and all other duties are kept informed about progress and decisions. Everyone uses good practices like decision-making, ownership, and clear communication to reach the Architecture milestone together.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: UI Development Specifications are clear instructions that explain how a website or app should look and work, including details like colors, buttons, and where things go on the screen, so that everyone knows what to build and how to make it easy and safe for people to use.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "How to Create UI Specifications": https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ui-specifications/
2. Smashing Magazine – "How To Create A Design Specification Document": https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2018/01/design-specification-document/
3. UX Collective – "How to create effective design handoff documentation": https://uxdesign.cc/how-to-create-effective-design-handoff-documentation-7b1f1b4b3f6c
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. UX Design duty gathers all the requirements by talking with Business Analysis and Product Ownership duties to make sure everything needed for the UI is clear and complete. 
2. UX Design duty creates detailed UI Development Specifications, including making annotations that explain how each part of the user interface should work and look, while getting advice from other duties like UX Research, Writing, Strategy, Product Management, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis. 
3. UX Design and Business Analysis duties check the specifications together to make sure they are correct and ready for development, while keeping all other duties informed about the progress and using good practices like making decisions, taking responsibility, helping others, making everyone feel safe to share ideas, and giving people the power to do their best work.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: UI Development Specifications are valuable because they give clear instructions for how a product should look and work, helping everyone understand what needs to be built. By creating annotations, people doing business analysis, product ownership, and UX research can make better decisions, take responsibility, and make sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. This helps reach the requirements milestone because everyone knows exactly what is needed and can work together to make the product the best it can be.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The UX (User Experience) Design duty owns the UI Development Specifications and works closely with Business Analysis and Product Ownership to make sure all requirements are clear and complete. UX (User Experience) Design is responsible for creating annotations and making sure the UI Development Specifications are ready for development, while Business Analysis and UX (User Experience) Design are accountable for the final results. UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Product Ownership, Product Management, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress. The work uses skills in UI Specifications for Development and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the requirements milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Unique Value Proposition is a clear statement that explains what makes a product special and why people should choose it over others, using information from market research and product positioning, and requires careful thinking, good communication, and understanding what customers need.
Online Resources:
1. HubSpot: "How to Write a Unique Value Proposition"  
https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/write-value-proposition

2. Harvard Business Review: "A Simple Value Proposition Can Be Your Most Powerful Tool"  
https://hbr.org/2016/10/a-simple-value-proposition-can-be-your-most-powerful-tool

3. Nielsen Norman Group: "Value Proposition: How to Write It and Deliver It"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/value-proposition/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by doing market research to learn what customers want and what other products are out there.  
2. Use product positioning to decide what makes your product special and how it stands out from others.  
3. Work together by sharing ideas, giving advice, and always learning new things to keep making your Unique Value Proposition better.  

To make a Unique Value Proposition, the Product Management duty leads and does most of the work, while other duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, and different UX duties help by sharing their knowledge and advice. Everyone stays updated and helps by using skills like problem-solving, communication, and making good decisions, and by always being open to new ideas and learning more.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Unique Value Proposition helps cross-functional duties by clearly showing what makes a product special and why people should care about it, which makes it easier for everyone to make smart choices, work together, and keep improving the product during Continuous Discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a Unique Value Proposition, the Product Management duty is in charge and makes sure everything gets done right, while also doing most of the work. Other duties like Business Analysis, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, and UX (User Experience) Writing help by giving advice and sharing their knowledge. All duties, including Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Operations, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept updated about what is happening. Everyone works together by doing activities like market research and product positioning, using skills such as problem-solving, teamwork, and communication, and following good practices like making decisions, taking ownership, and helping each other feel safe to share ideas. The main milestone is continuous discovery, which means always learning and improving the Unique Value Proposition.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A Usability Test Report explains what was learned from watching people use a product, shows what problems they had, and gives clear ideas for making the product easier and better to use.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – Usability Test Reporting: What? Why? How?  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/usability-test-reporting/

2. Interaction Design Foundation – How to Write a Usability Test Report  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/how-to-write-a-usability-test-report

3. UX Mastery – How to Write a Usability Report  
https://uxmastery.com/how-to-write-a-usability-report/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Plan the usability test by making a research plan and deciding what to test and how to test it, with advice from other duties like Product Management and UX Design. 
2. Run the usability test by watching real people use the product, taking notes, and collecting feedback to see what works well and what needs fixing. 
3. Analyze the research findings, write the Usability Test Report to explain what was learned, and share the results with all duties, using good communication, critical thinking, and teamwork, while making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas and keeps learning.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A Usability Test Report is valuable because it shows what real people think and do when using a product, helping everyone doing their duty understand what works well and what needs to be fixed, so they can make better decisions and keep improving the product together to reach the goal of continuous discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The duty owner, UX (User Experience) Research, works with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, UX (User Experience) Design, and UX (User Experience) Strategy to complete a Usability Test Report by planning and running usability tests, analyzing research findings, and sharing results. UX (User Experience) Research is accountable and responsible for making sure the Usability Test Report is done well, while Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing are consulted to give advice and feedback. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are informed about the progress and results. Everyone uses skills like critical thinking, teamwork, communication, and decision-making, and follows practices like ownership, psychological safety, and continuous growth to reach the milestone of Continuous Discovery.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: Use Case Diagrams are pictures that show how people or things use a system, helping everyone understand what the system should do by showing different actions and who does them, and making sure all the important needs and rules are clear before building or improving the system.
Online Resources:
1. Lucidchart - Use Case Diagram Tutorial  
https://www.lucidchart.com/pages/uml-use-case-diagram

2. Visual Paradigm - How to Draw Use Case Diagram  
https://www.visual-paradigm.com/guide/uml-unified-modeling-language/what-is-use-case-diagram/

3. IBM Developer - UML Use Case Diagrams: Guidelines  
https://developer.ibm.com/articles/the-use-case-diagram/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Business Analysis duty gathers information and writes clear acceptance criteria, making sure everyone understands what the Use Case Diagram needs to show. 
2. Business Analysis uses problem-solving, communication, and organization skills to draw the Use Case Diagram, asking for advice from duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture. 
3. Business Analysis shares the diagram with Product Ownership for approval, keeps other duties like QA Analysis, Project Management, Product Operations, Information Security Analysis, and Graphic Design updated, and follows good practices like making decisions together, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so the Use Case Diagram is correct and meets the requirements.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Use Case Diagrams are valuable because they show how people and systems interact with a product, making it easier for everyone with a duty to understand what needs to be built and why. This helps people doing different duties agree on what the product should do, spot problems early, and make better decisions together, which helps everyone reach the requirements milestone faster and with fewer mistakes.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create Use Case Diagrams, the Business Analysis duty leads the work by making sure all the right steps are followed and the details are clear, while Product Ownership checks and approves the final results. Business Analysis is responsible for writing acceptance criteria and using skills like problem-solving, communication, and organization. Other duties, like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge. Many other duties, such as Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress and decisions. Everyone uses good practices like making decisions together, taking ownership, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas, so Use Case Diagrams are done well and meet the requirements.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Workshop That Produces the Deliverable: "Audience Segmentation, Goals, and Use Cases Workshop"
Deliverable Description: Use Case Scenarios are clear stories that explain how people will use a product or system, and they include writing down what is needed, setting rules for when something is finished, and making sure everyone understands the steps, so the right thing gets built and works well for everyone.
Online Resources:
1. Interaction Design Foundation – "Use Cases: What They Are and How to Write Them"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/use-cases-what-they-are-and-how-to-write-them

2. Lucidchart – "How to Write a Use Case"  
https://www.lucidchart.com/blog/how-to-write-a-use-case

3. AltexSoft – "How to Write Effective Use Cases"  
https://www.altexsoft.com/blog/business/how-to-write-effective-use-cases/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The Business Analysis duty writes clear requirements and acceptance criteria by talking with other duties to understand what is needed and making sure everything is correct and complete. 
2. The Business Analysis duty asks for advice and feedback from duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture to make the Use Case Scenarios better. 
3. All duties stay updated about the progress, use good practices like making decisions together, taking responsibility, helping each other, feeling safe to share ideas, and making sure everyone can do their best, so the Use Case Scenarios are useful and easy to understand.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Use Case Scenarios are valuable because they help everyone doing a duty understand exactly how people will use what is being made, so each duty can make better choices, work together, and make sure the final product meets what is needed to reach the goal.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: For Use Case Scenarios, the Business Analysis duty owner works with other duties by writing requirements and creating acceptance criteria, using skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and clear communication. Business Analysis and Product Ownership are accountable for making sure Use Case Scenarios are correct and complete, while Business Analysis is responsible for doing the work. Duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and feedback. All duties, including Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress. Everyone uses practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the requirements milestone and make sure the Use Case Scenarios are useful and clear.
