Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: A User Journey Map shows the steps a customer takes when using a product or service, using research and customer information to help people understand what customers need, what problems they face, and how to make their experience better.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – User Journey Mapping: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/user-journey-mapping/
2. Interaction Design Foundation – Journey Mapping 101: https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/journey-mapping-101
3. Adobe XD Ideas – How to Create a Customer Journey Map: https://xd.adobe.com/ideas/process/user-research/customer-journey-maps-beginners-guide/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by doing research to learn about customers, using both interviews and information from books or the internet.  
2. Group customers into different types based on what they need and like, and map out each step they take when using a product or service.  
3. Share ideas, make decisions together, and check that all information is correct, while asking for advice from others and keeping everyone updated, using good communication, problem-solving, and making sure everyone feels safe to share their thoughts.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A User Journey Map is valuable because it shows how customers experience a product or service step by step, helping everyone with duties like research, design, and writing understand what customers need and where they might have problems, so they can make better decisions and keep improving things all the time.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create a User Journey Map, the duties of UX (User Experience) Design, Product Management, and UX (User Experience) Research work together by sharing ideas, doing research, and making decisions at each step, while Business Analysis and Product Management are accountable for making sure everything is correct and finished. UX (User Experience) Strategy, Product Management, and UX (User Experience) Research are responsible for doing the main work, like mapping customer experiences and gathering information. Other duties, such as UX (User Experience) Writing, Product Ownership, Scrum Master, Development, Graphic Design, Solutions Architecture, and Information Security Analysis, are consulted to give advice and help solve problems. All duties, including QA Analysis, Project Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress and updates. Everyone uses skills like teamwork, problem-solving, and communication, and follows practices like decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety to reach the milestone of Continuous Discovery.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: User Personas are detailed descriptions of different types of customers, created by using customer segmentation and research, to help people making decisions understand who they are designing for and what those customers need, so they can make better choices and create products that work well for everyone.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "Personas: A Simple Introduction"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/persona/

2. Interaction Design Foundation – "Personas – A Simple Introduction"  
https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/personas-why-and-how-you-should-use-them

3. Adobe XD Ideas – "How to Create Detailed User Personas"  
https://xd.adobe.com/ideas/process/user-research/how-to-create-detailed-user-personas/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by using Customer Segmentation to group users by their needs and behaviors, with Product Management making sure everything is done correctly and leading most of the work.
2. Get advice and information from Product Ownership and UX Research duties about what users want and how they use the product, while also asking for ideas and feedback from UX Design, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Business Analysis, and Development duties to make the User Personas more accurate.
3. Keep everyone updated on progress, use problem-solving, good communication, and helping others, and always keep learning about users through Continuous Discovery to make the User Personas better.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: User Personas are valuable because they help everyone understand who the customers are, what they need, and what problems they face, so people doing different duties can make better choices and create things that really help customers, which is important for always learning and improving during Continuous Discovery.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create User Personas, the Product Management duty is in charge and makes sure everything gets done right, while also doing most of the work. Product Ownership and UX (User Experience) Research duties help by giving advice and sharing what they know about users and products. Other duties like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Business Analysis, and Development are asked for their ideas and feedback to make the User Personas better. All the other duties are kept up to date about how the User Personas are coming along. Everyone works together by using skills like problem-solving, teamwork, and good communication, and they follow practices like making good decisions, taking responsibility, helping others, and making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas. The main activity is Customer Segmentation, and the big milestone is Continuous Discovery, which means always learning more about users.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: User Story Statements are clear and simple sentences that explain what a user needs and why, created by gathering information, asking questions, and writing down the details so everyone understands what should be built and how it should work.
Online Resources:
1. Atlassian Agile Coach – User Stories  
https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/user-stories

2. Mountain Goat Software – User Stories  
https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/agile/user-stories

3. Scrum.org – Writing Good User Stories  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/writing-good-user-stories
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Gather information by talking to people who will use or work with the product and write down what they need and want. 
2. Work with others who give advice, like people who design, research, or build the product, to make sure the information is clear and correct, and organize it so everyone understands. 
3. Write user story statements that explain what the user needs in a simple way, check with everyone to make sure it is right, and keep everyone updated about any changes or progress, using good listening, clear writing, and problem-solving skills while making sure everyone feels safe to share ideas and help make decisions.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: User Story Statements are valuable because they help everyone understand what needs to be built, why it matters, and how it should work, making it easier for people with different duties to work together and reach the requirements milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete User Story Statements, the Business Analysis duty is accountable and responsible for gathering and refining requirements, writing user stories, and making sure everything is clear and organized, while Product Ownership also shares accountability by helping set priorities and direction. Other duties like UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Writing, UX (User Experience) Strategy, Product Management, Scrum Master, Account Management, Development, and Solutions Architecture are consulted to give advice and feedback, making sure the User Story Statements meet everyone’s needs. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX (User Experience) Design, UX (User Experience) Research, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about progress and changes. The work involves activities like requirements gathering, user story generation, and writing requirements, using skills such as meeting facilitation, negotiation, attention to detail, collaboration, and problem-solving, while following practices like decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to reach the requirements milestone.
Required Activities: Requirements Gathering,Requirements Refinement,User Story Generation,Writing Requirements

Deliverable Description: A UX Audit Report is a document that explains how easy and enjoyable it is for people to use a website or app, using facts and careful checks to find problems and suggest ways to make it better for everyone, including people with different abilities.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – "UX Audit: How to Conduct a Heuristic Evaluation"  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ux-audit-heuristic-evaluation/

2. UX Collective – "How to Conduct a UX Audit: A Step-by-Step Guide"  
https://uxdesign.cc/how-to-conduct-a-ux-audit-a-step-by-step-guide-8e2c2a1b6c2a

3. CareerFoundry – "How to Conduct a UX Audit (With a Free Template)"  
https://careerfoundry.com/en/blog/ux-design/how-to-conduct-a-ux-audit/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Start by collecting analytics, which means gathering data about how people use the product, like what pages they visit and where they click.  
2. Next, do a UX heuristics analysis by checking the product against a list of good design rules to find any problems that make it hard to use.  
3. The UX Research duty is in charge of these steps, but other duties like Product Ownership, Product Management, UX Strategy, UX Design, Business Analysis, and UX Writing help by giving advice and sharing what they know, while everyone else is kept updated about what is happening and what is found.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A UX Audit Report is valuable because it helps everyone understand how people use a product, what problems they face, and what can be improved, so different duties like business analysis, product management, and UX design can make better decisions, work together, and create a safer and more helpful experience for users, which helps reach important goals.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To complete a UX Audit Report, the UX (User Experience) Research duty is the owner and is both accountable and responsible for collecting analytics and doing UX heuristics analysis, using skills like attention to detail, critical thinking, and teamwork. Other duties, such as Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Strategy, UX (User Experience) Design, Business Analysis, and UX (User Experience) Writing, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, while all other duties are kept informed about the progress and results. Everyone works together by practicing decision-making, ownership, service leadership, psychological safety, and empowerment to meet the requirements milestone and make sure the UX Audit Reports are accurate and helpful.

Deliverable Description: A Vision Board is a clear picture of what you want to achieve in the future, showing your big ideas and goals for a project. Making Vision Boards means you need to think carefully about what you want, listen to feedback from others, and make smart choices. You use skills like planning, being open to new ideas, and making sure everyone feels safe to share their thoughts. Vision Boards help guide your actions and keep you focused on what matters most.
Online Resources:
1. MindMovies – Vision Board Tutorials and Examples  
https://www.mindmovies.com/blogroll/vision-board-tutorials

Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Product Management leads everyone in creating Vision Boards by starting the Release Vision Definition activity, using skills like planning releases, listening to feedback, thinking ahead, and forming a clear vision. 
2. Duties like UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, Business Analysis, and Development share their ideas and help shape the Vision Boards by giving advice and suggestions. 
3. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, stay updated on progress and decisions, working together with good habits like being flexible, making choices, taking responsibility, helping others, feeling safe to share, and feeling empowered, so the Vision Boards show a clear and shared direction for everyone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Vision Boards are valuable because they help everyone understand what the group wants to achieve and why it matters, making it easier to make good choices, share ideas, and feel safe to speak up, which helps everyone work together to reach the Vision milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: Product Management is accountable for Vision Boards and is also responsible for making sure the Vision Boards are created by leading the Release Vision Definition activity, using skills like Release Management, Receiving Feedback, Strategic Thinking, and Vision Formation. Other duties, such as UX Design, UX Research, UX Writing, UX Strategy, Product Ownership, Business Analysis, and Development, are consulted to give their ideas and help shape the Vision Boards. All duties, including Business Analysis, Development, Graphic Design, Information Security Analysis, Product Management, Product Operations, Product Ownership, Project Management, QA Analysis, Scrum Master, Solutions Architecture, UX Design, UX Research, UX Strategy, UX Writing, Account Management, and Agile Coaching, are kept informed about the progress and decisions. Everyone works together using practices like Agility, Decision-Making, Ownership, Service Leadership, Psychological Safety, and Empowerment to reach the Vision milestone and make sure the Vision Boards show a clear and shared direction.

Deliverable Description: Wireframes are simple drawings that show what a website or app will look like and how people will use it, and making them means you need to create clickable models, test different versions, organize information clearly, and use creative thinking and good communication to make sure everything is easy to understand and works well for everyone.
Online Resources:
1. Nielsen Norman Group – Wireframe Examples and Tutorials  
https://www.nngroup.com/articles/wireframe-definition/

2. Adobe XD Ideas – How to Create Wireframes: A Step-by-Step Guide  
https://xd.adobe.com/ideas/process/wireframing/how-to-create-wireframes/

3. UX Design Institute – How to Create Wireframes: A Beginner’s Guide  
https://www.uxdesigninstitute.com/blog/how-to-create-wireframes/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. Start by talking with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, and Product Ownership to understand what users need and what the goals are. 
2. Use hard skills like drawing and using design tools to make simple sketches, called Wireframes, that show what the website or app will look like and how people will use it. 
3. Share these Wireframes with consulted duties, get feedback, and make changes to improve them, then create clickable prototypes and test them with users to see what works best, making sure to keep everyone informed about the progress and results.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Wireframes are valuable because they help everyone see and agree on how a website or app will look and work before building it, making it easier to spot problems early, share ideas, and make smart choices to reach the Experience Design milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the Wireframes, the duty owner, which is UX (User Experience) Research and UX (User Experience) Design, works closely with other duties like Business Analysis, Product Management, Product Ownership, and UX (User Experience) Strategy by sharing ideas, testing prototypes, and making sure the design matches what users need. The duties that are accountable make sure the Wireframes are correct and meet the goals, while the responsible duties actually do the work like drawing, testing, and improving the Wireframes. Consulted duties, such as Product Ownership, Product Management, UX (User Experience) Writing, Development, and others, give advice and feedback to help make the Wireframes better. Informed duties, including Development, Graphic Design, QA Analysis, and many more, are kept up to date about the progress and results so everyone knows what is happening. All these duties use skills like problem-solving, teamwork, attention to detail, and communication, and they follow good practices like making decisions together, taking ownership, and making sure everyone feels safe to share their ideas.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Workshop That Produces the Deliverable: Working Agreements Workshop,Conflict Resolution Training Workshop,Agile Product and Service Development Lifecycle Training Workshop,Agile and Service Leadership Workshop
Deliverable Description: Working Agreements are a set of clear rules and promises that everyone follows to help people work well together, solve problems, make good decisions, and feel safe and respected while doing their duties.
Online Resources:
1. Scrum.org – "Working Agreements in Scrum Teams"  
https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/working-agreements-scrum-teams

2. Atlassian – "Team Working Agreements: How-to Guide"  
https://www.atlassian.com/team-playbook/plays/working-agreements

3. Mountain Goat Software – "How to Create Team Working Agreements"  
https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/how-to-create-team-working-agreements
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable:  
1. Gather everyone with different duties to talk about how they want to work together and share their ideas.  
2. Use good communication, listening, and problem-solving skills to agree on rules for working, making decisions, and making sure everyone feels safe and included.  
3. Write down the Working Agreements, check that all duties understand and agree, and keep everyone updated as you reach the Intake milestone.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: A set of Working Agreements is valuable because it helps everyone doing different duties understand how to work together, share ideas, and solve problems in a fair and respectful way, which makes it easier to reach the Intake milestone.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: To create the Working Agreements, the Product Operations duty is responsible for making sure all the steps are done, while the Scrum Master, Agile Coaching, and Product Operations duties are accountable for the final result. Many other duties, like Product Management, UX Design, Development, and QA Analysis, are consulted to share their ideas and help solve problems. All duties are kept informed about the progress and decisions. The activities include defining how everyone will work together, using skills like negotiation, communication, and problem-solving. Important practices such as decision-making, ownership, and psychological safety help everyone feel included and empowered as they reach the Intake milestone.

Data Type Description (from Data Type): Something provided as a result of a process.
Deliverable Description: The Workshop Results are what you get after leading Agile workshops, where you help people share ideas, solve problems, and make decisions together by using good listening, clear talking, and fair ways to work out disagreements, all while making sure everyone feels safe and included so the group can keep learning and improving.
Online Resources:
1. Harvard Business Review – "How to Present Workshop Results"  
https://hbr.org/2016/06/how-to-present-to-senior-executives

2. Miro Blog – "How to Run a Workshop: Step-by-Step Guide"  
https://miro.com/blog/how-to-run-a-workshop/

3. SessionLab – "How to Facilitate a Workshop"  
https://www.sessionlab.com/blog/how-to-facilitate-a-workshop/
Steps to Produce Deliverable:
Steps to produce the deliverable: 
1. The Scrum Master makes sure everyone is ready and sets up the Agile workshop, using skills like leading meetings, solving problems, and helping everyone work well together. 
2. People with duties in Product Management, UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development share their ideas and advice, while everyone else is kept up to date about what is happening and what needs to be done. 
3. Everyone practices good talking, making choices together, and taking care of their own work, reaching important points like Continuous Discovery and Experience Design, so the Workshop Results are clear, helpful, and everyone understands the next steps.
Why is this deliverable valuable?: Workshop Results are valuable because they show what everyone learned and decided together, making it easier for people with different duties to understand what to do next and work better together. This helps everyone keep discovering new ideas and designing better experiences, so the group can reach their goals faster and solve problems as they come up.
How To Work Cross-Functionally On the De: The Scrum Master is the duty owner and is accountable and responsible for Workshop Results, making sure all the activities like facilitating Agile workshops and using skills such as meeting facilitation, problem-solving, and teamwork happen smoothly. Other duties, like Product Management, UX Design, Business Analysis, and Development, are consulted to give advice and share their knowledge, while all duties are kept informed about the progress and outcomes. Everyone works together by practicing good communication, decision-making, and ownership, and by reaching important milestones like Continuous Discovery and Experience Design, so Workshop Results are successful and everyone knows what is happening and what needs to be done.
